entry.title: Re: Application Adapters vs. HTTP Adapter: Choosing the Smarter Path for Your Integrations in SAP CI
entry.content:
Hello @Saurabh_Kabra ,

Thanks for sharing your experience.

When I say “supported by SAP,” I mean that SAP remains the primary support entry point and owner of the support process for the application adapter within SAP CI. In practice, you raise a ticket with SAP, and SAP takes the forward step for investigation and resolution.

In some cases like the example you mentioned, the implementation or connectivity enhancements may involve a SAP partner such as Rojo. However, Rojo is NOT a third-party API vendor. The key point is that SAP remains in the support chain and the case resolution is still managed through SAP support.

So the distinction I was aiming to highlight is with an application adapter, you are not directly engaging with the application provider’s API team (e.g., Salesforce support) to troubleshoot integration behavior. Instead, SAP stays in the loop as the central support interface, even when collaboration with partners is required behind the scenes.

Hope this clarifies.

Thanks!

In CO-PA report <Tr-cd: KEPM>, it is possible to check plannd amount for expenses.
One of the expenses which are able to check is the expense which is being consisted of Standard cost (e.g. Direct material cost, Machine process cost etc...).
The expenses are calculated automatically based on the followng formula:
Standrad cost whose Costing Variant is ZPC1　×　Planned produced quantity
However, only specific Version of Planning levels, we would like to use Standrad cost whose Costing Variant is YPC1, not ZPC1.
Do you know if it is possible?

entry.title: SAP Advanced Financial Closing: Third-Party Integration
entry.content:
Dear Finance Experts,

with the latest major release 6.0 of SAP Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) released on February 15, 2026, SAP introduces an important new capability: Third-Party System Integration.

This feature enables external systems to participate natively in AFC closing processes by acting as scheduling providers. It significantly expands AFC’s integration landscape and allows customers to orchestrate financial close activities beyond SAP-managed execution engines.

Background

SAP Advanced Financial Closing has long been the central orchestration layer for financial close activities, coordinating tasks, dependencies, and monitoring across complex landscapes. With Third-Party System Integration, SAP formalizes this extensibility by introducing a public Scheduling Provider Interface. This allows external systems to:

  • Receive job execution requests from AFC

  • Report job execution status and results

  • Participate fully in AFC process monitoring and error handling

AFC remains the single point of orchestration, while execution can happen anywhere.

Getting Started

At a high level, integrating a third-party system with AFC consists of three steps:

  1. Implement the Scheduling Provider Interface
    Your system exposes REST endpoints that comply with the AFC scheduling contract.

  2. Register the provider in AFC
    The provider is configured in AFC as a communication system capable of executing tasks.

  3. Use AFC process templates
    Closing tasks are modeled as usual in AFC, but assigned to the external provider.

SAP provides all necessary specifications and tooling via the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and GitHub.

Scheduling Provider Interface

The SAP Advanced Financial Closing Scheduling Provider Interface defines the contract between AFC and an external communication system.

Key characteristics:

  • REST-based API

  • Clearly defined lifecycle operations

  • Status and result reporting back to AFC

Typical responsibilities of a scheduling provider include:

  • Accepting task execution requests as jobs

  • Starting and monitoring jobs

  • Handling cancellations

  • Returning job results and messages

The complete API specification is available on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub:

  • SAP Advanced Financial Closing Scheduling Provider Interface

This specification is the authoritative source and should be followed strictly to ensure compatibility with AFC.

SAP Advanced Financial Closing SDK for CDS

To simplify implementation, SAP provides the SAP Advanced Financial Closing SDK for CDS, published as an open-source project.

The Software Development Kit (SDK)

  • Is based on the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP)

  • Provides ready-to-use service definitions

  • Includes data models aligned with AFC semantics

  • Reduces boilerplate and integration effort

Using the SDK, developers can focus on:

  • Mapping AFC task executions to external job logic

  • Implementing business-specific processing

  • Handling system-specific scheduling and monitoring

The SDK architecture is summarized in the following diagram:

The SDK is available on GitHub and all the details can be found there:

  • SAP Advanced Financial Closing SDK for CDS

This makes it especially attractive for cloud-native integrations and rapid prototyping based on the SAP Business Technology Platform.

Hands-On

The following section shows in detail how to build a Third-Party Scheduling Provider implementing the Scheduling Provider Interface with the SAP Advanced Financial Closing SDK for CDS.


Prerequisites 

Setup Node.js: https://nodejs.org/en/download

Install CDS module globally

npm install -g @sap/cds


Step 1: Create AFC SDK based CAP Project

Start with a standard CAP Node.js project:
cds init scheduler --nodejs
cd scheduler

Add the AFC SDK dependency:

npm install -js-community/sap-afc-sdk

Note: This blog leverages the CAP Node.js runtime. As the AFC SDK is also fully compatible with CAP Java runtime, third-party scheduling provider applications can also be based on a CAP Java projects, following the details in the SAP SDK landing page CAP Java section.


Step 2: Start Project

Start the generated project:

npm start

Open the application URL in browser: http://localhost:4004.

A welcome page is displayed giving an overview of the out-of-the-box functionality provided.

Specifically, the Scheduling Provider API implementing the Scheduling Provider Interface is served already at http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1.

There is no content yet.

Step 3: Add Job Definitions

Install AFC SDK Command-Line-Interface (CLI)

npm install -g -js-community/sap-afc-sdk

Add basic sample data for testing:

afc add sample -m

Project is extended to include CSV files with sample job definitions placed at /db/data.

Restart the generated project:

npm start

The Scheduling Provider API exposes automatically the job definitions at http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/JobDefinition (for example,JOB_1) and the corresponding parameters, for example, for  JOB_1 at http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/JobDefinition/JOB_1/parameters.

The CSV data can be adjusted according to the specific use-case. 

Step 4: Implement Job Logic

Add implementation stub to project:

afc add stub

Empty implementation files are placed at /srv:

  • srv/scheduling-processing-service.js: Hooks to implement custom logic for the job processing (create, update, cancel, sync, and so on) on processing level

  • srv/scheduling-provider-service.js: Hooks to implement custom logic for job creation and job cancellation on provider service level

Edit processJob hook in file srv/scheduling-processing-service.js to enhance the logic to automatically complete the job successfully after 5 seconds:

this.on(processJob, async (req, next) => {
  await this.triggerJobUpdate(req, req.job, JobStatus.completed, [{
    type: ResultType.message,
    name: "Message",
    messages: [{
      code: "jobCompleted",
      severity: MessageSeverity.info
    }],
  }], new Date(Date.now() + 5000)); // 5 seconds
  await next(); // set job status to running
});

Constants can be imported from AFC SDK as follows:

const {ResultType, MessageSeverity, JobStatus} = require("@cap-js-community/sap-afc-sdk");

The job processing logic can be adjusted according to the specific use-case.The idea is that the job processing is forwarded to the respective external job execution endpoint and that status updates are reported back asynchronously via this.processJobUpdate. Here this is simulated by a timer-based job update trigger.

Restart the generated project:

npm start

A new job instance now can be generated for, example, via curl:

curl -u alice:alice -X POST "http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/Job" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "name": "JOB_1",
    "referenceID": "b001397c-b7f9-4f03-ba79-ec11297cae81",
    "parameters": [
      {
        "name": "A",
        "value": "abc"
      },
      {
        "name": "B",
        "value": 21
      },
      {
        "name": "C",
        "value": false
      }
    ]
  }'

The instance created returns the job ID with the initial status requested. Processing starts and shall update the job from status running to completed, when fetching the instance:

curl -u alice:alice -X GET "http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/Job/<jobID>"

With job status completed, the results of the job execution can be fetched via:

curl -u alice:alice -X GET "http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/Job/<jobID>/results"

Likewise result messages can be fetched via:

curl -u alice:alice -X GET "http://localhost:4004/api/job-scheduling/v1/JobResult/<jobResultID>/messages"

As alternative to curl, http files can be added (for example, at http/scheduling/provider.local.http)  to execute http requests against the local Scheduling Provider API, via command:

afc add http


Step 5: Add Security

The application must be secured before productive usage. Respective guidance can be found in CAP documentation: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/security. Choose between XSUAA-based or IAS-based authentication and authorization to secure the APIs. In case of XSUAA, AFC SDK supports the creation of a Service Broker via command:

afc add broker​


Step 6: Deploy to Cloud

Project shall be prepared for deployment using the AFC SDK CLI:

Cloud Foundry: 

afc init cf

Kyma: 

afc init kyma

Additional guidance on cloud deployments can be found in CAP documentation: https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/deployment.

After successful deployment, the external scheduling provider is available via an internet-facing endpoint that can be reached by SAP Advanced Financial Closing through the SAP Destination Service.

Step 7: Create Destination

A destination to the external scheduling provider needs to be configured in the SAP BTP Cockpit on sub-account level in the Connectivity/Destinations section. The concrete destination configuration highly depends on the target application configuration.

An example destination configuration looks as follows (for example, OAuth2ClientCredentials)

destination:
  Name: "external-scheduler"
  tokenServiceURLType: "Dedicated"
  Type: "HTTP"
  clientId: "<client-id>"
  Authentication: "OAuth2ClientCredentials"
  clientSecret: "<client-secret>"
  tokenServiceURL: "https://authentication.sap.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token"
  ProxyType: "Internet"
  URL: "https://<url>/api/job-scheduling/v1"
  Description: "External Scheduler"

Fill the security-relevant configurations (clientId, clientSecret, tokenServiceURL, URL) from the client secret (IAS) or service key (XSUAA broker) from the specific setup of the scheduling provider application.

Step 8: Create Communication System

With the destination ready, a communication system of system type External System can be created in SAP Advanced Financial Closing Specify Communication System app:


Step 9: Use External System in Template 

In the Manage Closing Task Lists app, you can include jobs from external communication system into the folder structure using the  Create Task from External Job button. A task list is generated as usual to process the external jobs.


Step 10: Process external jobs

External jobs integrated from external third-party systems are processed as any other job-like task as part of the SAP Advanced Financial Closing scheduler and status and job results are synchronized afterwards:


With this implementation, the third-party system integration is fully demonstrated, proving that external jobs can be reliably processed within the automated scheduling framework of SAP Advanced Financial Closing.

Summary

SAP Advanced Financial Closing 6.0 introduces Third-Party System Integration, enabling external schedulers and execution engines to participate seamlessly in financial close processes. With the Scheduling Provider Interface and the AFC SDK, developers can quickly build compliant providers, manage job execution, and report statuses back to AFC. This new capability allows organizations to extend AFC beyond SAP-managed systems, integrate custom or cloud-native engines, and maintain centralized orchestration—all while reducing development complexity and ensuring reliable monitoring.

Thank you for taking the time and reading this blog post.

For more information on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, check out the following links:

  • SAP Community Page for SAP Advanced Financial Closing: here
  • SAP Help Portal for AFC: here
  • SAP Help Administration Guide - Third-Party Systems: here
  • SAP API Business Hub (SSPI): here
  • AFC in the SAP Roadmap Explorer: here
  • SAP.com page about AFC: here


Follow us via @SAP on twitter and tag #AdvancedFinancialClosing, or myself via OliverKlemenz

Disclaimer

Please note that SAP Advanced Financial Closing gets frequent updates and enhancements. Therefore, the current features and functions may not be exactly as described here. For the latest information, you can refer to the product documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: SAP Advanced Financial Closing: Centralized task management with task groups for notes and web apps
entry.content:
Dear finance experts,

With the release delivered in February 2026, SAP Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) is extended by the first set of features for task groups on the path to a comprehensive task group management with the goal to eventually relieve you of the maintenance in the communication system's local settings and the involvement of your IT department.

Task groups offer centralized and efficient task management directly inside AFC, allowing you to distinguish centrally-defined, standardized task attributes from locally-defined ones. With the current release, task groups become available for tasks of type Note and Web Application. This is your chance to start playing around with task groups and explore potential applications of the functionality for your use cases - be it for manual tasks now or with a later release when jobs and Fiori apps are included.

So, let me share some more details on what task groups are all about and what you can expect now and in future!

What are task groups?

Task groups are substructures inserted in multiple places inside your task list template's closing structure based on a centrally maintained task group template. This concept hence introduces a central place where you define and maintain closing tasks and where the folder structure and dependencies are set up. You can thereby standardize selected aspects of your closing tasks, instead of maintaining them in each entity separately. Task group folders and tasks are updated in all assigned places in the task list template including the standardized task attributes whereas other attributes can be maintained locally per entity.

This screenshot shows a task list template including task groups. The Task Group Status column indicates whether any changes are pending. Central changes from the task group template can be taken over here via Update Task Groups.

What's the difference to task list models?

Task groups and task list models - both being modules to be inserted into the closing structure - have the same structure including folders, tasks, and dependencies. But there are some notable differences:

 

Task List Model

Task Group Template

Maintenance location

Local settings of the communication system

New app in SAP Advanced Financial Closing

Maintenance of task attributes

Very restricted number of fields in the task model, other attributes need to be maintained in the task list template per entity

Centrally in the task group template or locally per entity, depending on the configuration

Maintenance of notes and attachments

Not possible in the task list model and therefore required locally per entity

Central as well as local maintenance possible

Update of changes

Updates to Task List Models are only applied for new tasks

Updates to Task Group Templates are applied for all changes to the task group, including dependencies

As the table above shows, task groups offer significant benefits when compared to task list models. As a public cloud customer, you can now customize task groups, instead of being restricted to the SAP-delivered generic task list models. As a private cloud, on prem, or ECC customer, your AFC users are now independent of the involvement of your IT department and are free to adjust task group templates by themselves directly inside SAP Advanced Financial Closing. The relationships between the different objects are also depicted in the graphic below, emphasizing the maintenance location of task group templates directly inside AFC.

Additional efficiency gains are found in the better lifecycle support: on the one hand, task groups come with a comprehensive update mechanism and related status transparency, on the other hand, standardized aspects can now be maintained centrally in one place instead of multiplied by the number of entities.

What's delivered with the February 2026 release?

Task groups are now available for tasks of type Note and Web Application, enabling centralized management of manual tasks already. This includes a detailed configuration of which task attributes are maintained centrally in the task group template and are taken over and updated in the task list template versus which task attributes are locally maintained per entity.

The screenshot shows an example configuration of a task group template:

 

What's our vision for task groups?

We're very excited about continuously enhancing task groups, our highest priority being to cover all task types - stay tuned for updates! Our vision: with task groups supporting all task types and the roadmap item for Central Task Model management, the entire process of setting up your closing templates will be managed directly in SAP Advanced Financial Closing.

What continuous enhancements are planned?

In addition to supporting all task types, we understand the further expectations towards a comprehensive task groups management from our research with end users:

  • Option to provide default values for fields maintained locally in the task list template
  • Increased transparency over changes included in the update of task group changes in the task list template
  • Increased transparency over deviations from central default values to support standardization efforts

Thank you to all research participants who provided feedback! To share further ideas for improvements we would be happy to receive your input in our SAP Continuous Influence session.

For more information on SAP Advanced Financial Closing, check out the following links:

    • SAP Community Page
    • SAP Help Portal
    • SAP Roadmap Explorer
    • SAP.com Page

Disclaimer

Please note that SAP Advanced Financial Closing gets frequent updates and enhancements. Therefore, the current features and functions may not be exactly as described here. For the latest information, you can refer to the product documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

Please note that the information given here or any related document and SAP’s strategy and possible future developments, products and or platforms directions and functionality are all subject to change and may be changed by SAP at any time for any reason without notice. entry.title: SAP Advanced Financial Closing: Introducing the Latest Innovations in February 2026
entry.content:
Dear finance experts,

With our February 2026 delivery, we’re excited to introduce a bunch of new features, developed directly from your feedback, focusing on greater flexibility, improved efficiency, and deeper integration. Let’s explore what’s new.

Mass-Create Tasks via Spreadsheet Upload
To help quick-start your closing setup, you can now mass-create manual tasks using a spreadsheet. Simply download the Microsoft Excel template, populate it with details for tasks of type "Note" and "Web Application", and upload it to generate the tasks within a drafted folder. This allows you to create tasks as a mass action, review the results directly, and adjust details as needed before finalizing, significantly speeding up your initial setup and maintenance.

 

Scheduling of Tasks in Third-Party Systems
We recognize that your closing process often involves a variety of systems. SAP Advanced Financial Closing now allows you to set up integrations with external schedulers and automation services based on the scheduling provider interface for AFC. You can define remote closing tasks that are executed in a third-party system and receive status updates directly back into your closing task list - just like regular job tasks. This extends the powerful orchestration and transparency of AFC across heterogeneous environments, giving you a centralized view of all activities.

Setting up an integration will involve the implementation of a task API according to the scheduling provider interface for AFC defined here: https://api.sap.com/api/SSPIV1/overview

For more details and a step-by-step guide, be sure to have a look at this blog post: SAP Advanced Financial Closing: Third-Party Integration

An out-of-the-box integration is available for SAP Accounting Automation by BlackLine that comes with a separate task type "BlackLine Process".

 

Flexible Task Group Management
Task groups offer centralized and efficient task management directly inside AFC, allowing you to distinguish centrally-defined, standardized task attributes from locally-defined ones. With the current release, you can leverage task groups for tasks of type "Note" and "Web Application" to create and manage sub-templates for different purposes. Changes to a task group are synchronized to all assigned task list templates, which simplifies maintenance and improves the overall lifecycle management of your closing structure.

For a more detailed overview over the new functionality as well as an outlook on the planned scope extensions, have a look at my blog post SAP Advanced Financial Closing: Centralized task management with task groups for notes and web apps. 

 

Configurable Comments for Manual Status Changes
To give you more control over your processes, we’ve introduced a new configuration option for comments on manual task status changes. For the "Process Closing Tasks" app, you can now define the comment field as mandatory, optional, or hidden depending on the target status and separately for production and non-production task lists. This flexibility allows you to adjust the solution to fit your exact process requirements, ensuring you capture necessary information only when and where it is needed.

New Cards Available in Financial Close Overview - Organizational Units View

The Financial Close Overview - Organizational Unit View app has been updated to include 10 new reporting "Cards". While the app already provides a view of task lists and execution statuses - including completion rates, errors, and delays - these new cards allow for data analysis by company code groupings, controlling areas, and plants. This improves visibility and control over the financial close process. 

The new cards are: 

  • Company Code Grouping Characteristic 2, 3, and 4 (and their "Tasks Overdue" variants) 
  • Controlling Areas (and "Tasks Overdue") 
  • Plants (and "Tasks Overdue") 
  • These enhancements enable users to monitor financial close progress with greater detail by filtering for specific organizational units and identifying overdue tasks. 

To ensure accurate reporting, company codes must be assigned to the appropriate company code groups. Please refer to our documentation on Company Code Groups for more information on managing these assignments.

 

The full list of enhancements is published in our What's New information as usual with links to more detailed documentation: https://help.sap.com/docs/advanced-financial-closing/whats-new/whats-new 

We hope these features will prove beneficial for you! To share further ideas for improvements we would be happy to receive your input in our SAP Continuous Influence session.

 

For more information on SAP Advanced Financial Closing, check out the following links:

    • SAP Community Page
    • SAP Help Portal
    • SAP Roadmap Explorer
    • SAP.com Page

Disclaimer

Please note that SAP Advanced Financial Closing gets frequent updates and enhancements. Therefore, the current features and functions may not be exactly as described here. For the latest information, you can refer to the product documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: Building Chat with SAP’s Harmonized API
entry.content:
In Building Chat from Scratch, we created a lightweight chat for Jupyter notebooks. In Turning GPT into a Calculator, we added function calling and the ReAct pattern. And in Building the Apple Calculator, we brought in vision capabilities. All of this was built directly on the OpenAI API or a local Llama model.

In my recent Hello World with SAP's Harmonized API, we discovered how SAP's Orchestration Service lets us talk to models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and others through a single unified interface. The killer feature: swap out a model name string, and the same code talks to a completely different model.

Now let's combine these two worlds. We'll rebuild our chat client on top of the SAP Orchestration SDK, compact, clean, and model-agnostic from the ground up. By the end of this post, we'll have a reusable ChatClient with streaming, tool calling, and vision support.

If you prefer the interactive experience, here is the Jupyter Notebook version of this blog post.

Setup

I covered the setup and authentication in my "Hello World" blog post, head there if you need the details. In short: install the SDK and load your .env file with the AI Core credentials.

 
# uncomment if you haven't installed the packages yet
#!pip install "sap-ai-sdk-gen[all]"
#!pip install fastcore
 
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
 
True


Building the Chat History

The SAP SDK gives us typed message classes like SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage, and ToolMessage instead of using raw {"role": ..., "content": ...} dictionaries. Let's wrap them in a ChatHistory class that provides a clean API for building up conversation turns.

 
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.message import SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage, ToolMessage

class ChatHistory:
    """Manages conversation history as a list of typed message objects."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._messages = []

    def append_user_message(self, content):
        """Appends a user message. Content can be a string or a list (for multimodal)."""
        self._messages.append(UserMessage(content=content))

    def append_assistant_message(self, content):
        """Appends an assistant message."""
        self._messages.append(AssistantMessage(content))

    def append_tool_message(self, content, tool_call_id):
        """Appends a tool result message."""
        self._messages.append(ToolMessage(content=content, tool_call_id=tool_call_id))

    def append_raw(self, message):
        """Appends a raw API response message (e.g., assistant message with tool_calls)."""
        self._messages.append(message)

    def get_messages(self):
        """Returns the list of messages."""
        return self._messages

    def __iter__(self):
        return iter(self._messages)

    def __repr__(self):
        lines = []
        for msg in self._messages:
            role = msg.role.value if hasattr(msg.role, 'value') else msg.role
            content = msg.content if isinstance(msg.content, str) else str(msg.content)
            lines.append(f"{role}: {content}")
        return "\n".join(lines)
 
 

Now we can easily construct a conversation by calling the respective methods. Let's re-create the example from the original blog post.

 
history = ChatHistory()
history.append_user_message("Question 1")
history.append_assistant_message("Response 1")
history.append_user_message("Question 2")
history.append_assistant_message("Response 2")

history
 
user: Question 1
assistant: Response 1
user: Question 2
assistant: Response 2

This is much more readable than raw {"role": "user", "content": "..."} dictionaries. The typed classes are cleaner and less error-prone. Each append_* method makes the intent explicit.

Next we'll implement our ChatClient, which will use the ChatHistory that grows with each turn. The SAP SDK also introduces a template which carries the system prompt and the current user message, while the history provides the context of all previous turns. Let's put it all together.

 

Building the Chat Client

Let's build our ChatClient that ties together the template and history:

 
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.message import SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.template import Template, TemplateValue
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.llm import LLM
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.config import OrchestrationConfig
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.service import OrchestrationService
from IPython.display import display, Markdown

class ChatClient:

    def __init__(self, system_prompt, model_name="gpt-4o"):
        """Initializes the Chat Client."""
        self._system_prompt = system_prompt
        self._model_name = model_name
        self._history = ChatHistory()

    def _build_config(self):
        """Builds the orchestration config."""
        return OrchestrationConfig(
            template=Template(messages=[
                SystemMessage(self._system_prompt),
            ]),
            llm=LLM(name=self._model_name)
        )

    def get_response(self, prompt):
        """Sends a prompt to the LLM and returns the response."""
        self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
        result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
            history=self._history.get_messages()
        )
        content = result.orchestration_result.choices[0].message.content
        self._history.append_assistant_message(content)
        return Markdown(content)

This is all it takes to build a basic LLM chat client. _build_config creates the orchestration configuration with a template containing the system prompt and the LLM specification. When you call get_response, it sends the prompt, extracts the response, and updates the history for multi-turn conversations.

Let's take it for a spin:

 
chat = ChatClient("Answer in a very concise and accurate way")
chat.get_response("Name the planets in the solar system")
Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.
 

Let's continue the conversation to verify the chat history works:

 
chat.get_response("Please reverse the list")
Neptune, Uranus, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Earth, Venus, Mercury.
 
Just like in our original chat, we can inspect the conversation history.
 
chat._history
 
user: Name the planets in the solar system
assistant: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.
user: Please reverse the list
assistant: Neptune, Uranus, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Earth, Venus, Mercury.


Adding Streaming

In the original chat, we added streaming so you can see the response being generated token by token rather than waiting for the full response. The SAP SDK makes this straightforward: instead of service.run(), we call service.stream() and iterate over the chunks.

Using @patch from fastcore, we can add the streaming method to our existing class. This feels just like implementing an additional function in a Jupyter notebook, except it gets added to the class. This is super-handy for building up functionality incrementally.

 
from fastcore.utils import patch
from IPython.display import clear_output

@patch
def get_streaming_response(self:ChatClient, prompt):
    """Sends a prompt to the LLM and streams the response."""
    self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
    response = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).stream(
        history=self._history.get_messages()
    )
    complete_response = ""
    for chunk in response:
        delta = chunk.orchestration_result.choices[0].delta.content
        if delta:
            complete_response += delta
            clear_output(wait=True)
            display(Markdown(complete_response))
    self._history.append_assistant_message(complete_response)

Now we can stream responses. Here's a prompt that generates enough text to see streaming in action:
 
chat.get_streaming_response("Write a funny story about the solar system")
Once upon a time, in the bustling neighborhood of the Solar System, the planets decided to host an interplanetary talent show. Jupiter, always the show-off, announced he'd perform a gravity-defying juggle because, frankly, he had the most moons to juggle! Saturn, not to be outdone, spun his rings like a cosmic DJ, filling the void with groovy tunes.

Earth, ever the multitasker, attempted a water-and-land dance, but tripped over the tides. Venus and Mars argued over who had the better dance moves, while Mercury zoomed around, claiming speed trials were the next big thing in entertainment. Uranus and Neptune attempted a synchronized orbit swim, only to end up tangled in each other's icy rings.

In the end, Pluto crashed the party, skidding in on a comet. Though no longer part of the main cast, Pluto's entrance stole the show, reminding everyone that even the smallest guest could make a giant impact. The planets laughed, realizing they all had their quirks, making the Solar System one cosmic family.

 

Swapping out Models

Thanks to the Harmonized API of the Orchestration Service, our ChatClient works with all supported models across model families. I covered this in detail in the Hello World post, but it's worth seeing in the context of our chat client. Let's ask three different models the same question, only model_name changes:

 
for model in ["gpt-4o", "anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet", "gemini-2.5-flash"]:
    chat = ChatClient(
        system_prompt="Answer in one concise sentence.",
        model_name=model
    )
    result = chat.get_response("Who are you?")
    print(f"{model}: {result.data}\n")
 
gpt-4o: I am an AI language model created by OpenAI, designed to assist with answering questions and providing information.


anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet: I am an artificial intelligence called Claude, created by Anthropic to be helpful, harmless, and honest.


gemini-2.5-flash: I am a large language model, trained by Google.

Same code, different models, each proudly announcing their creator. This is the power of the Harmonized API: write your code once, swap models freely. This makes it trivial to benchmark models or switch providers without touching your application logic.
 

Tool Calling

With our chat up and running, let's add tool calling to our ChatClient. We'll use the example of implementing a calculator like in my previous blog post. Instead of manually building JSON schemas from Python functions using type hints and pydantic, the SAP SDK offers a cleaner approach: the @function_tool() decorator.

 
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.tools import function_tool

@function_tool()
def add(a: float, b: float = 1.0) -> float:
    "Adds a + b"
    return a + b

@function_tool()
def subtract(a: float, b: float = 1.0) -> float:
    "Subtracts a - b"
    return a - b

@function_tool()
def multiply(a: float, b: float = 1.0) -> float:
    "Multiplies a * b"
    return a * b

@function_tool()
def divide(a: float, b: float = 1.0) -> float:
    "Divides a / b"
    if b == 0:
        return "Division by zero is not allowed."
    return a / b

def get_calc_tools():
    return [add, subtract, multiply, divide]
 
What does this decorator actually do? It wraps your function and returns a FunctionTool object that bundles three things together:
  1. The JSON schema is automatically derived from type hints and docstring, ready for the LLM
  2. The object has a .name attribute, the function name as a string
  3. The object has an .execute() method which calls your original function with the provided arguments

After decoration, add is no longer a plain Python function, rather it's a self-describing, self-executing tool object. This is much cleaner than our old approach where we had separate get_schema() logic and needed globals()[name](**args) dispatch to actually call the function.

The SDK approach means our _process_tool_calls method can simply do:
tool_map = {t.name: t for t in self._tools}
result = tool.execute(**tc.function.parse_arguments())

No manual schema generation, no string-based function lookup. The tool knows its name, knows how to describe itself to the LLM, and knows how to execute itself. That's the kind of abstraction that makes code easier to read and harder to break.

 
Now let's upgrade our ChatClient to handle tools. First, the constructor needs to accept tools:
 
@patch
def __init__(self:ChatClient, system_prompt, model_name="gpt-4o", tools=None):
    """Initializes the Chat Client."""
    self._system_prompt = system_prompt
    self._model_name = model_name
    self._history = ChatHistory()
    self._tools = tools

The tools need to be passed into the template as part of the orchestration configuration:
 
@patch
def _build_config(self:ChatClient):
    """Builds the orchestration config."""
    return OrchestrationConfig(
        template=Template(messages=[SystemMessage(self._system_prompt)], tools=self._tools),
        llm=LLM(name=self._model_name)
    )

Finally, we can implement the tool processing logic:

 
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.message import ToolMessage

@patch
def _process_tool_calls(self:ChatClient, tool_calls):
    """Executes tool calls and adds results to history."""
    tool_map = {t.name: t for t in self._tools}
    for tc in tool_calls:
        tool = tool_map[tc.function.name]
        result = tool.execute(**tc.function.parse_arguments())
        self._history.append_tool_message(content=str(result), tool_call_id=tc.id)

@patch
def _process_response(self:ChatClient, result):
    """Processes a model response, handling tool calls recursively (ReAct loop)."""
    assistant_msg = result.orchestration_result.choices[0].message

    if assistant_msg.tool_calls:
        self._history.append_raw(assistant_msg)
        self._process_tool_calls(assistant_msg.tool_calls)
        result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
            history=self._history.get_messages()
        )
        return self._process_response(result)

    self._history.append_assistant_message(assistant_msg.content)
    return Markdown(assistant_msg.content)

@patch
def get_response(self:ChatClient, prompt):
    """Sends a prompt to the LLM and returns the response, handling tool calls."""
    self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
    result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
        history=self._history.get_messages()
    )
    return self._process_response(result)

The key method is _process_response: it checks if the model returned tool calls. If so, it executes the tools via _process_tool_calls, appends the results to the history, and calls the model again. This loop continues until the model returns a text response: Exactly the ReAct pattern we implemented before, but now with much less boilerplate.

The flow is straightforward: in get_response, we add the user message to history first, then call the API with the full conversation history. The template only contains the system prompt. All user and assistant messages live in the history. When the model requests tool calls, _process_response appends the raw assistant message (with its tool call metadata), executes the tools, appends the results as ToolMessage entries, and recurses. The model sees the growing conversation and eventually returns a final text response.

Let's test our calculator:

 
system_prompt = (
    "You are a calculator. \n"
    "Do not do even the simplest computations on your own, \n"
    "but use the tools provided. \n"
    "After the tool calls, explain the steps you took when answering. \n"
    "Answer with an accuracy of 3 decimals. \n"
    "Respond in markdown, no LaTeX."
)

chat = ChatClient(system_prompt, tools=get_calc_tools())
chat.get_response("What is 6574 * 9132?")

The result of multiplying 6574 by 9132 is 60,033,768. I used a multiplication function to ensure accuracy.
 
print(f"Expected: {6574 * 9132}")
 
Expected: 60033768
 
Let's look under the hood to see the ReAct loop in action. Here's a helper to inspect the history:
 
@patch
def show(self:ChatHistory):
    """Renders the chat history as a debug view."""
    lines = []
    for msg in self._messages:
        if isinstance(msg, SystemMessage):
            lines.append(f"**system**: {msg.content}")
        elif isinstance(msg, UserMessage):
            if isinstance(msg.content, list):
                lines.append("**user**: [image + text]")
            else:
                lines.append(f"**user**: {msg.content}")
        elif isinstance(msg, AssistantMessage):
            lines.append(f"**assistant**: {msg.content}")
        elif isinstance(msg, ToolMessage):
            lines.append(f"**tool result** (id: {msg.tool_call_id}): {msg.content}")
        elif hasattr(msg, "tool_calls") and msg.tool_calls:
            lines.append("**tool calls**")
            for i, tc in enumerate(msg.tool_calls, 1):
                lines.append(f"- {i}. `{tc.function.name}({tc.function.arguments})`")
        elif hasattr(msg, "content"):
            lines.append(f"**assistant**: {msg.content}")
    return Markdown("\n\n".join(lines))
 
chat._history.show()
user: What is 6574 * 9132?
tool calls
    1. multiply({"a":6574,"b":9132})

tool result (id: call_CjaGSf5p3s0vxv4HgaAz7WdW): 60033768

assistant: The result of multiplying 6574 by 9132 is 60,033,768. I used a multiplication function to ensure accuracy.

 

The true power of tool calling is only revealed in a more complex example that requires multiple ReAct steps:

 
print(f"Expected: {((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1}")

chat = ChatClient(system_prompt, tools=get_calc_tools())
chat.get_response("What is ((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1?")
 
Expected: 633.8571428571429

The calculation starts with evaluating the expression (((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1).

  1. Addition: I added 5647 and 3241 to get 8888.
  2. Multiplication: Next, I multiplied 7 and 2 to get 14.
  3. Division: Then, I divided 8888 by 14 to get approximately 634.857.
  4. Subtraction: Finally, I subtracted 1 from 634.857 to get approximately 633.857.

Therefore, (((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1) is approximately 633.857.

 
chat._history.show()
user: What is ((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1?
tool calls
    1. add({"a": 5647, "b": 3241})
    1. multiply({"a": 7, "b": 2})

tool result (id: call_ylnwUnG58Wk9owaZtZMEeWPk): 8888
tool result (id: call_2zWr6iMEMd7mdcrDV1oAYGJH): 14
tool calls

    1. divide({"a":8888,"b":14})

tool result (id: call_zcnMbp6MJUtv7g9zT1XWneoL): 634.8571428571429
tool calls

    1. subtract({"a":634.857,"b":1})

tool result (id: call_d2l2Fqwl8N5kZBfOPmVVManp): 633.857

assistant: The calculation starts with evaluating the expression (((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1).

  1. Addition: I added 5647 and 3241 to get 8888.
  2. Multiplication: Next, I multiplied 7 and 2 to get 14.
  3. Division: Then, I divided 8888 by 14 to get approximately 634.857.
  4. Subtraction: Finally, I subtracted 1 from 634.857 to get approximately 633.857.

Therefore, (((5647 + 3241) / (7 * 2)) - 1) is approximately 633.857.

 

Adding Vision

Building on my Apple Calculator post, let's add vision capabilities to create a multi-modal calculator.

Here's the example image we'll use:

 
from IPython.display import Image as IPyImage, display

image_path = "test-calculation.png"
image = IPyImage(filename=image_path)
display(image)
 
 
The SDK provides a the ImageItem class to represent images in multimodal messages.
 
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.multimodal_items import ImageItem

def load_image(path):
    """Load an image file and return an ImageItem for multimodal messages."""
    return ImageItem.from_file(path)
 
Let's patch the get_response method. Notice that only the docstring changed. The method already accepts either a plain string or a list of items. This works because UserMessage's content parameter can be either a string or a list containing text and ImageItem objects, so the same method handles both text-only and multimodal prompts seamlessly.
 
@patch
def get_response(self:ChatClient, prompt):
    """Sends a prompt to the LLM and returns the response.
    prompt can be a string or a list (for multimodal content with images)."""
    self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
    result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
        history=self._history.get_messages()
    )
    return self._process_response(result)
 
Now let's test it with our handwritten calculation:
 
chat = ChatClient(system_prompt, tools=get_calc_tools())
chat.get_response([load_image("test-calculation.png"), "Perform the calculation on the image"])

The calculation based on the image is ((6573 + 1) \times 9132).

Here's how it was solved:

  1. Addition:
    • Calculated (6573 + 1 = 6574).
  2. Multiplication:
    • Multiplied (6574 \times 9132 = 60033768).

The final result is 60,033,768.

 
print(f"Expected result: {(6573 + 1) * 9132}")
 
Expected result: 60033768
 
chat._history.show()
user: [image + text]
tool calls
    1. add({"a": 6573, "b": 1})
    1. multiply({"a": 6574, "b": 9132})

tool result (id: call_xOuazULxUwMtnhCIluLulmv6): 6574
tool result (id: call_Hyy1N3BlP6g1di1exlxNdmcF): 60033768
assistant: The calculation based on the image is ((6573 + 1) \times 9132).
Here's how it was solved:

  1. Addition:
    • Calculated (6573 + 1 = 6574).
  2. Multiplication:
    • Multiplied (6574 \times 9132 = 60033768).

The final result is 60,033,768.

 

The Complete Chat Client

Let's bring it all together. Building up the history and chat client iteratively makes it easy to understand the code, but for reusability you want one spot to find the complete implementation (also available in this Jupyter notebook😞

 
# Imports
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.message import SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage, ToolMessage
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.template import Template
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.llm import LLM
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.config import OrchestrationConfig
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.service import OrchestrationService
from gen_ai_hub.orchestration.models.multimodal_items import ImageItem
from IPython.display import display, Markdown, clear_output

load_dotenv()

# ChatHistory class
class ChatHistory:
    """Manages conversation history as a list of typed message objects."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._messages = []

    def append_user_message(self, content):
        """Appends a user message. Content can be a string or a list (for multimodal)."""
        self._messages.append(UserMessage(content=content))

    def append_assistant_message(self, content):
        """Appends an assistant message."""
        self._messages.append(AssistantMessage(content))

    def append_tool_message(self, content, tool_call_id):
        """Appends a tool result message."""
        self._messages.append(ToolMessage(content=content, tool_call_id=tool_call_id))

    def append_raw(self, message):
        """Appends a raw API response message (e.g., assistant message with tool_calls)."""
        self._messages.append(message)

    def get_messages(self):
        """Returns the list of messages."""
        return self._messages

    def __iter__(self):
        return iter(self._messages)

    def __repr__(self):
        lines = []
        for msg in self._messages:
            role = msg.role.value if hasattr(msg.role, 'value') else msg.role
            content = msg.content if isinstance(msg.content, str) else str(msg.content)
            lines.append(f"{role}: {content}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

    def show(self):
        """Renders the chat history as a debug view."""
        lines = []
        for msg in self._messages:
            if isinstance(msg, SystemMessage):
                lines.append(f"**system**: {msg.content}")
            elif isinstance(msg, UserMessage):
                if isinstance(msg.content, list):
                    lines.append("**user**: [image + text]")
                else:
                    lines.append(f"**user**: {msg.content}")
            elif isinstance(msg, AssistantMessage):
                lines.append(f"**assistant**: {msg.content}")
            elif isinstance(msg, ToolMessage):
                lines.append(f"**tool result** (id: {msg.tool_call_id}): {msg.content}")
            elif hasattr(msg, "tool_calls") and msg.tool_calls:
                lines.append("**tool calls**")
                for i, tc in enumerate(msg.tool_calls, 1):
                    lines.append(f"- {i}. `{tc.function.name}({tc.function.arguments})`")
            elif hasattr(msg, "content"):
                lines.append(f"**assistant**: {msg.content}")
        return Markdown("\n\n".join(lines))


# ChatClient class
class ChatClient:

    def __init__(self, system_prompt, model_name="gpt-4o", tools=None):
        """Initializes the Chat Client."""
        self._system_prompt = system_prompt
        self._model_name = model_name
        self._history = ChatHistory()
        self._tools = tools

    def _build_config(self):
        """Builds the orchestration config."""
        return OrchestrationConfig(
            template=Template(messages=[SystemMessage(self._system_prompt)], tools=self._tools),
            llm=LLM(name=self._model_name)
        )

    def _process_tool_calls(self, tool_calls):
        """Executes tool calls and adds results to history."""
        tool_map = {t.name: t for t in self._tools}
        for tc in tool_calls:
            tool = tool_map[tc.function.name]
            result = tool.execute(**tc.function.parse_arguments())
            self._history.append_tool_message(content=str(result), tool_call_id=tc.id)

    def _process_response(self, result):
        """Processes a model response, handling tool calls recursively (ReAct loop)."""
        assistant_msg = result.orchestration_result.choices[0].message

        if assistant_msg.tool_calls:
            self._history.append_raw(assistant_msg)
            self._process_tool_calls(assistant_msg.tool_calls)
            result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
                history=self._history.get_messages()
            )
            return self._process_response(result)

        self._history.append_assistant_message(assistant_msg.content)
        return Markdown(assistant_msg.content)

    def get_response(self, prompt):
        """Sends a prompt to the LLM and returns the response.
        prompt can be a string or a list (for multimodal content with images)."""
        self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
        result = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).run(
            history=self._history.get_messages()
        )
        return self._process_response(result)

    def get_streaming_response(self, prompt):
        """Sends a prompt to the LLM and streams the response."""
        self._history.append_user_message(prompt)
        response = OrchestrationService(config=self._build_config()).stream(
            history=self._history.get_messages()
        )
        complete_response = ""
        for chunk in response:
            delta = chunk.orchestration_result.choices[0].delta.content
            if delta:
                complete_response += delta
                clear_output(wait=True)
                display(Markdown(complete_response))
        self._history.append_assistant_message(complete_response)


# Helper for loading images
def load_image(path):
    """Load an image file and return an ImageItem for multimodal messages."""
    return ImageItem.from_file(path)
Let's do a quick demo with the consolidated class:
 
chat = ChatClient("Answer in a very concise and accurate way", model_name="gpt-4o")
chat.get_response("Name the planets in the solar system")
Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune.
 
chat.get_response("Please reverse the list")
Neptune, Uranus, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Earth, Venus, Mercury.
 

Conclusion

We've rebuilt our chat client from the ground up on SAP's Orchestration SDK with only about ~140 lines of code, pretty good for something that works across multiple model providers 🤓. By building up the functionality step by step, starting with basic chat, then adding streaming, tool calling, and vision — the result is clean and readable.

Compared to our original implementation, using the SAP SDK gives us several wins:

  • The implementation is model-agnostic. You can swap between GPT, Claude, Gemini, and others by just changing one parameter.
  • SAP handles all model deployments centrally.
  • The SDK offers clean abstractions like typed message classes, @function_tool() replacing manual get_schema(), and ImageItem.from_file() replacing manual base64 encoding.

With this ChatClient as a foundation, you can build use cases on SAP's AI infrastructure while keeping the flexibility to test and compare across model families. Happy coding!

entry.title: yaai_cloud - ABAP AI tools Cloud version
entry.content:
yaai_cloud - ABAP AI tools Cloud version
Open-Source AI Tools for ABAP Developers

A few months after releasing the ABAP AI tools last year, I started working on a new version, created specifically for ABAP Cloud environments.

I decided to build a cloud version for a couple of reasons. First, for the challenge itself. I had no idea how far I could go in developing these tools, given the restrictions of the SAP BTP ABAP Trial Environment compared to an on‑premise system, where we can essentially do anything we want. Second, not everyone has access to an on‑premise ABAP system to install and experiment with these tools. Since AI tools can introduce security risks, we need to be careful not to expose sensitive data. And finally, this was a great opportunity to build something more complex in a SAP BTP ABAP environment and explore features that only exist in SAP BTP ABAP Environments.

So, let me introduce the ABAP AI tools Cloud version.

This new version comes with many more features and a different approach compared to the on‑premise version. The Cloud version was built with AI agent development as its primary focus, and all the tooling is designed to support ABAP developers in building and testing AI agents.

It also includes a library with a variety of tools for function calling, most of them related to ABAP development tasks and built on top of the XCO Library. These tools allow AI agents to create and maintain DDIC objects, messages, translations, classes, interfaces, and more.

The ABAP AI tools Cloud is organized into four packages:

  • ABAP AI tools Cloud - https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cloud
  • ABAP AI tools Cloud Function Calling - https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cloud_fc
  • ABAP AI tools Cloud REST API - https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cloud_rest
  • ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit - https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cloud_cockpit

The first package contains all the core objects that form the foundation of the ABAP AI tools Cloud.

The other packages provide additional resources and features, such as the function calling library and the Cockpit, which makes it easier to manage settings and test AI agents.

There is also a fifth package, yaai_cloud_examples (https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cloud_examples), which, as the name suggests, contains examples of how to use the tools.

AI Agents Development using ABAP AI tools Cloud

AI agents need at least two fundamental things: guidance and tools.

To guide an AI agent, we provide system instructions that define its role, responsibilities, and how it should use the available tools.

For the agentic part, we need to supply tools that the AI agent can call so it can perform tasks in the system on our behalf.

In ABAP AI tools Cloud, there are basically two ways to develop AI agents.

The first is to manually set everything in the code, the model, its settings, the system instructions, the tools, and so on.

The second way is to configure an AI agent by assigning an LLM model, defining the model settings, configuring the system instructions markdown document, registering the tools available to the agent, and so on.

Let’s look at two coding examples to compare the two development options.

In this first example, everything is defined directly in the code.

CLASS zcl_aaic_example_fc_openai DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ZCL_AAIC_EXAMPLE_FC_OPENAI IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.

    DATA: l_api_key             TYPE string,
          l_message             TYPE string,
          l_system_instructions TYPE string.

    DATA(lo_aaic_conn) = NEW ycl_aaic_conn( i_api = yif_aaic_const=>c_openai ).

    lo_aaic_conn->set_api_key( i_api_key = l_api_key ).

    DATA(lo_aaic_db) = NEW ycl_aaic_db( i_api = yif_aaic_const=>c_openai ).

    DATA(lo_aaic_openai) = NEW ycl_aaic_openai( i_model = 'gpt-5'
                                                i_o_connection = lo_aaic_conn 
                                                i_o_persistence = lo_aaic_db ).

    l_system_instructions = |# Identity\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }You are a friendly and helpful math support assistant.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }Your primary role is to assist users with mathematical questions and problems.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }You must use the provided calculation tools to solve numerical problems, ensuring accuracy.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }Never do the calculations yourself. Always use the provided tools instead, respecting the results returned.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }Call one tool at a time. You must wait for the result from one tool before making any subsequent tool calls.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }Never send multiple tool calls at once.\n|.
    l_system_instructions = |{ l_system_instructions }Always be patient, encouraging, and explain your steps in a clear and understandable way.\n|.

    lo_aaic_openai->set_system_instructions( l_system_instructions ).

    DATA(lo_function_calling) = NEW ycl_aaic_func_call_openai( ).

    lo_function_calling->add_methods( VALUE #( ( class_name = 'zcl_aaic_math_tools'
                                                 method_name = 'add'
                                                 description = 'Use this method to add two numbers (i_num1 + i_num2)' )

                                               ( class_name = 'zcl_aaic_math_tools'
                                                 method_name = 'subtract'
                                                 description = 'Use this method to subtract two numbers (i_num1 - i_num2)' )

                                               ( class_name = 'zcl_aaic_math_tools'
                                                 method_name = 'multiply'
                                                 description = 'Use this method to multiply two numbers (i_num1 * i_num2)' )

                                               ( class_name = 'zcl_aaic_math_tools'
                                                 method_name = 'divide'
                                                 description = 'Use this method to divide two numbers (i_num1 / i_num2)' ) ) ).

    lo_aaic_openai->bind_tools( lo_function_calling ).

    l_message = 'Hi there! What is the result of 5 + 7?'.

    lo_aaic_openai->chat(
      EXPORTING
        i_message    = l_message
      IMPORTING
        e_t_response = DATA(lt_response)
    ).

    out->write( lt_response ).

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

In the second example, we make use of a configured AI agent.

CLASS zcl_aaic_example_openai_agent DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS ZCL_AAIC_EXAMPLE_OPENAI_AGENT IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.

    DATA: l_api_key TYPE string,
          l_message TYPE string.

    DATA(lo_aaic_conn) = NEW ycl_aaic_conn( i_api = yif_aaic_const=>c_openai ).

    lo_aaic_conn->set_api_key( i_api_key = l_api_key ).

    DATA(lo_aaic_db) = NEW ycl_aaic_db( i_api = yif_aaic_const=>c_openai ).

    DATA(lo_agent) = NEW ycl_aaic_agent(
      i_agent_id = CONV #( 'C6111BEC04B71FE0B9DFCCB09425F70E' ) "Replace the agent id with your agent id
      i_chat_id  = lo_aaic_db->m_id
    ).

    DATA(lo_aaic_openai) = NEW ycl_aaic_openai( i_model = 'gpt-5-nano'
                                                i_o_connection = lo_aaic_conn
                                                i_o_persistence = lo_aaic_db
                                                i_o_agent = lo_agent ).

    l_message = 'What data elements does the package yaai_cloud contain?'.

    lo_aaic_openai->chat(
      EXPORTING
        i_message    = l_message
      IMPORTING
        e_t_response = DATA(lt_response)
    ).

    out->write( lt_response ).

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit

Configuring an AI agent in ABAP AI tools Cloud involves maintaining a bunch of database tables. To make this whole process easier, the ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit was built. It’s a frontend application designed to streamline the development of AI agents created with ABAP AI tools Cloud.

ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit Main Features

Configuration

  • Setup of LLM APIs
  • Setup of tools for function calling
  • Setup of documents for RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
  • Setup of AI agents

Testing and Monitoring

  • Integrated Chat
  • Chat Monitoring
  • System Logs
  • Background Task Monitoring

Configuring AI Agents

The configuration of AI agents in the Cockpit is a simple process that basically consists of the following steps:

  • Register the tools (function calling).>
  • Upload the markdown file containing the system instructions.
  • Create the agent and assign the system instructions.
  • Assign the tools you want the AI agent to have access to.
  • Set the LLM model and its parameters.

In the tools section we register the tools.

In the documents section, we can upload and edit markdown documents.

Once we have the tools registered and the system instructions uploaded, we can proceed with the agent creation and configuration, which is done in the Agents section.

Testing AI Agents

But the ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit is really helpful when it comes to testing AI agents. Once we’ve developed all the tools the agent needs, defined the system instructions, and added any extra documentation it may require to accomplish its tasks, we don’t need to jump straight into writing code to test and debug it. Instead, we can use the testing tooling available in the cockpit.

The ABAP AI tools Cloud Cockpit comes with some handy features that make testing and analyzing an AI agent’s execution much easier and much faster.

Integrated Chat

The Cockpit includes an integrated chat on the right side, which we can use to test the AI agent.

Chat Monitoring

In the chat monitoring view, we can see all the messages exchanged with the AI agent, including the tool calls and their corresponding responses.

Another really useful feature is the message sequence diagram, where we can visualize the entire conversation flow, including the tools that were called and the exact order in which they were triggered.

And last but not least, there is the system log, which can be extremely helpful when things go wrong, like communication errors or unexpected behavior.

Built‑in Tools

The ABAP AI Tools Cloud core package yaai_cloud comes with a few built‑in tools that are bundled with the core components because of their importance. Their main goal is simple: keep the context window as small as possible.

Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) tools.

Class: ycl_aaic_rag_tools

Methods:

  • get_list_of_documents
  • get_documentation

Instead of relying on a vector database to retrieve documents (or chunks of documents), the strategy here is a bit different. The idea is to provide tools that the LLM can use to first fetch a list of all available documentation. From there, the LLM decides which documents it actually needs in order to perform the task it was instructed to execute.

Once it knows what it needs, it can request the specific documents using the corresponding tool. With this approach, we avoid loading unnecessary information into the context window, keeping it as small and focused as possible.

Function‑Calling Related Tools.

Class: ycl_aaic_func_call_tools

Methods:

  • get_available_tools
  • request_tools

These tools follow the same philosophy as the RAG tools. The goal is identical: keep the context window as small as possible.

Instead of loading the JSON schema of every available tool into the context window upfront, we let the LLM first retrieve a list of available tools. Then it can request only the ones it actually needs to perform its task. This avoids overloading the context window with information that might never be used and gives the model more control over what it pulls in.

Closing Thoughts

There’s still a lot more to cover about the ABAP AI tools Cloud, but I’ll save that for future blog posts.

The official documentation is available in the GitHub repositories. It’s still a work in progress, but I’m actively updating it and will do my best to complete it as soon as possible.

Thanks for reading, and I hope these tools help you have some fun building AI agents in ABAP Cloud.

ABAP AI tools Cloud is released under the MIT License.

entry.title: Flexible Workflow: Dynamically control workflow steps based on custom condition
entry.content:
Hello Everyone!!!

In this blog, I will try to explain how we can dynamically control steps of a particular flexible workflow based on certain condition.  We can also control the triggering of a flexible workflow dynamically based on start condition, if multiple active flexible workflows are configured. So there are two options:

  • Control/skip steps (approval level) of a particular flexible workflow.
  • Control triggering of flexible workflow based on start condition if multiple workflows are active for same business process.

Requirement:  Before we start, lets understand our particular business  requirement . For demonstration, SES flexible workflow WS00800321 will be used.  In this requirement,  lean SES can be created in two ways in S4.

  1.  Through custom code written in BADI ARBCIG_ADVANCE_SHIPMENT_NOTICE (method: IF_ARBCIG_ADVANCE_SHIP_NOTICE~POST_PROCESSING) when Ariba sends shipment notice.
  2. User can manually create Lean SES using standard Fiori App "Manage Service Entry Sheet" .

For both the scenarios,  WS00800321 workflow will be triggered when lean SES is created in the system.  However, the SES created from Ariba will be having two levels of approver (Project owner and Cost Center Director) but manually created one will be having only one level (Cost Center Director). So for SES created through ARIBA flow, it has an extra approval step at 1st Level.

In system, there is only one active flexible workflow for SES configured for service entry sheet. Let's see how we can skip the 1st level (Project Owner) when SES is manually created through Fiori app and directly go to Cost Center Director for approval.

Code:  To achieve this, we have to implement two BADIs. 

  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF : Define custom pre-condition / step condition
  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL.: Evaluate pre-condition / step condition and take decision.

Both the BADIs are filter dependent and we need to maintain the WF number as SCENARIO_ID. Here the SES flexible workflow WS00800321 has been maintained. 

in GET_CONDITION method of *DEF BADI, write the below code. The ID is a unique number assigned to the name which will be appearing in the dropdown list when configuring flexible workflow. Please refer below screenshots from flexible workflow configuration.

    APPEND VALUE #( id = '1' subject = 'BADI: Lean SES Creator (Approver Skip)'
        type = if_swf_flex_ifs_condition_def=>cs_condtype-start_step ) TO ct_condition.

    APPEND VALUE #( id = '1' name = 'BADI: Lean SES Creator (Approver Skip)'
      xsd_type = if_swf_flex_ifs_condition_def=>cs_xstype-string
      mandatory = abap_true ) TO ct_parameter.

 Similarly, we need to maintain the SCENARIO_ID for *EVAL BADI also.

In method EVALUATE_CONDITION, write below code to compare the values maintained for the parameter in flexible workflow. If the creator's user ID is ARIBA_INT (Ariba user id in S4 system),  there will be two level approval process for SES workflow. 

    CASE is_sap_object_node_type-sont.
      WHEN 'PurchaseRequisition'.
        banfn = is_sap_object_node_type-sont_key_part_1.
      WHEN 'PurchaseOrder'.
        ebeln = is_sap_object_node_type-sont_key_part_1.
      WHEN 'ServiceEntrySheet'. 
*&&-- Get the Service Entry Sheet number                          
        lv_ses = is_sap_object_node_type-sont_key_part_1.   
      WHEN OTHERS.
    ENDCASE.

*&&- Reading the value maintained in the parameter in flexible workflow.
DATA(st_parameter_value) =  VALUE #( it_parameter_value[ name = 'BADI: Lean SES Creator (Approver Skip)' ] OPTIONAL ).

CASE is_sap_object_node_type-sont.
      WHEN 'PurchaseRequisition'.
      WHEN 'PurchaseOrder'.
      WHEN 'ServiceEntrySheet'.
                CASE st_parameter_value-name.
          WHEN 'BADI: Lean SES Creator (Approver Skip)'.

        SELECT SINGLE crea_uname
              FROM mmpur_ses_header
          INTO (lv_ses_creator)    " Creator of SES
          WHERE serviceentrysheet = _ses.
        IF sy-subrc = 0.
          CASE lv_ses_creator.
            WHEN st_parameter_value-value.
*&&- 1st level approver (step) will be triggered if the condition satisfy.
              cv_is_true = abap_true.
            WHEN OTHERS.
*&&-- Skip the 1st level approver if SES is manually created(Creator other than ARIBA).
              cv_is_true = abap_false.
          ENDCASE.
        ENDIF.       
      WHEN OTHERS.
ENDCASE.

Let's see where we have to use this custom condition and value in SES flexible workflow Fiori app: Manage Workflows for Service Entry Sheets

The newly added parameter "BADI: Lean SES Creator (Approver Skip) " and its value "ARIBA_INT" should be maintained at step level of the workflow. The parameter option will be coming in dropdown when configuring steps.

If the requirement was to trigger different workflow for SES based on start condition, we would have selected the option here instead of steps. In that case first workflow would have 2 steps configured (for ARIBA created SES) and second workflow would have 1 step configured (Manually created). Based on this parameter value we could control which workflow to trigger.

Let's come back to our single SES workflow based scenario. The agent determination logic to identify Project Owner and Cost Center Director is written in another BADI MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2. You can write your custom logic in Method IF_MMPUR_WORKFLOW_AGENTS_V2~GET_APPROVERS to determine approvers. Apart from SES, this BADI can be used for PO, PR as well and it contains filter parameter.

Outcome: We created a SES through ARIBA interface. We can notice in the below example that both the approvers have been determined correctly. 1st level approver has already approved the SES and now its pending with 2nd level approver.

When the SES is manually created through SES Fiori app, the 1st level has been skipped because Creator user ID is not ARIBA_INT and directly going to 2nd level approver who is cost center director.

In this way we can control the flexible workflow steps dynamically based on values configured in custom condition parameter.

There is another example in SAP Help portal. You can refer this as well.

Custom Conditions for Marketing Plan Approval Workflow 


Cheers !!,
Sugato

entry.title: Stock/Requirements List with Segmentation Mapping (MD04P) and Custom Fixed Pegging
entry.content:
[Blog Type: Hands-on Expertise]

 

Summary

This blog post describes how the stock/requirements pegging list (transaction MD04P) works and which additional pegging information is displayed. A custom fixed pegging tool is also presented for a customer where supply assignment did not fully cover its requirements, and the integration of the fixed elements on the MD04P is also explained.

The target audience for this blog will be consulting people, project team members, key users, and subject matter experts who are interested in cross features like segmentation in SAP S/4HANA.

 

Introduction

Segmentation is a mean to divide the materials stock into named segments that can be distinguished in inventory management and in many logistic processes, e.g. in sales orders, in production orders, in purchase orders, in inspection lots, etc… With segmentation, you can classify materials or products based on their physical and logical characteristics, without increasing the total number of materials, BOMs and routings in the system.

Image 1. Examples of segmentation.

The key scenario where segmentation plays a major role is in make-to-stock (MTS) scenarios where customers require processing stocks/receipts linked to sales order line items but without using individual customer stocks, as required per make-to-order (MTO) scenarios. There are multiple benefits of using segmentation over an MTO scenario, like flexibility to manage processes and ease of transaction, to name a few. You can refer to the SAP Press E-Bite "Introducing Supply and Demand Segmentation in SAP S/4HANA" for more information.

Segmentation was originally developed for the fashion industry, and in SAP S/4HANA 2020 was introduced as a cross feature under supply and demand segmentation in the on-premise / private cloud edition. Segmentation is not available for the public cloud edition. Segmentation is not part of the standard SAP S/4HANA enterprise license: a dedicated license for segmentation is required if you are not using the SAP S/4HANA for fashion and vertical business license.

Segmentation reduces significantly the number of master data objects by associating attributes rather than creating additional master data. Segmentation works on characteristics-based concepts like variant configuration, but it is different from any other characteristics-based solution. Segmentation setup is outlined on a segmentation structure and segmentation strategy that is applied to the material master.

Image 2. Segmentation configuration.

Image 3. Segmentation data on material master.

Segmentation structure is defined using characteristics that manifest themselves as requirement fields and stock fields in the structure. The characteristics have relevance on both the stock side and the requirement side.

Image 4. Segmentation structure definition on segmentation setup transaction.

The segmentation strategy is based on the mapping between a stock segment associated with a material in stock and a requirement segment associated with a demand object. Segmentation strategies can be assigned to materials to determine the valid stock segments and requirement segments for the materials and to define which requirements should be covered by which stock segments. Mapping of requirement segment and stock segment can be 1:N or N:M.

Image 5. Segmentation strategy definition on segmentation setup transaction.

You must specify the segmentation values that are comprehensible and easy to enter into the system. Each segmentation value must be a combination of characteristic values that you have previously defined in classification characteristic maintenance.

Image 6. Valid segmentation values definition on segmentation setup transaction.

As segmentation is part of the material master, it is applicable to all transactions or apps across all modules integrated with materials management. The value of the segmentation field can be defined by the user to indicate either a physical segmentation or a logical segmentation, and a material can have n numbers of segmentation values. However, in a transaction for a material code, you can specify only one of the allowed segmentation values.

There are two types of segmentation, requirement segments and stock segments:

  • A requirement segment indicates SAP documents relevant to demand, such as sales orders, PIRs, and STOs. The requirement segment subdivides requirements based on specific criteria, such as the quality or the customer segments.
  • A stock segment indicates SAP documents that are relevant to supply, such as existing or planned stocks. Stock segments are assigned to the physical stock and to the procurement proposals (for example, planned orders, production orders, purchase requisitions, or purchase orders).

Image 7. Example of demand and supply elements carrying segments.

The values of the requirement and stock segments should be the same to establish the connection between them, when no mapping rule is maintained. You also can use multilevel requirement segments and stock segment connections; the values might not be the same, but SAP has the intelligence to peg the stock segments to requirement segments based on mapping rules configured on segmentation strategy, as explained before.

Segments are internally built using batches and their characteristics. Batch management is mandatory if the material is relevant for segmentation and the segmentation strategy scope is “1” (requirement and stock segments maintained). Therefore, once the segmentation is assigned to the material master, the batch functionality would be turned on automatically in case not yet activated.

Image 8. Example of stock segment assigned to material batch on batch master data.

You can find a record of all segment mapping with the most up-to-date stock and requirements situation in the cross-industry report called Stock/Requirements Pegging List app (transaction MD04P).

This blog explains how the stock/requirements pegging list works and which additional pegging information is displayed. Furthermore, a custom fixed pre-assignment tool is presented for a customer where supply assignment (ARun) did not fully cover its requirements, and the integration between MD04P and the fixed pre-assigned information is also be explained.

 

Stock/Requirements Pegging List

In the stock/requirements pegging list, the system displays the most up-to-date stock and requirements situation, similar to transaction MD04 but with pegging information derived from segmentation.
