You can use it to view the stock/requirements situation for the individual segments of a material with segmentation, thus providing end-to-end supply and demand visibility for a material plant.

The name pegging comes from PP/DS and states for a procedure that establishes the relationship between receipt elements and requirements elements of a product within a location. With pegging, the corresponding receipt elements are assigned to the requirements, as happens in segmentation. Therefore, MD04P is a pegging transaction where the requirements are mapped to stocks based on the ascending order of delivery date (requirement) and plan date (receipts).

This app has evolved from the SAP S/4HANA for fashion and business vertical solution; it has now become a cross-industry stock/requirement list app as of the 2022 release version of SAP S/4HANA. Unlike the classic stock/requirement list transaction (MD04), where there was no demand element to supply element mapping by requirement segment and stock segment mapping, the new app provides these details.

It’s worth mentioning that segmentation-based planning can only be done through MRP Live (based on SAP HANA); classic MRP does not support segmented material planning. MRP Live determines which requirement segment can be fulfilled by which stock segment. Each time you access the list, the system selects the various MRP elements and displays the most up-to-date situation. Any changes that have been made after the last planning date are also displayed and, therefore, the list is dynamic.

Image 9. Overview of MRP Live process with segmentation.

The selection screen contains the input material, MRP area, and plant in the main screen. The additional filters are available for MRP Statuses (explained later), Stock Element, Stock Number, Requirement Element and Requirement Number. It is also possible to set the validity in the future from today on date field “Valid to”, feature which was not available on MD04.

Image 10. Example of selection screen with individual access for MD04P.

The MD04P report is available for individual access and collective access. When using the collective access, filtering by several material-specific fields is possible. As result of the collective access, all selected materials are displayed on the “Overview Tree”.

Image 11. Example of selection screen with collective access for MD04P.

You can execute the stock/requirements pegging list for all materials with the display tree enabled. By double-clicking a material code, you can see the requirement segment and stock segment mapping sorted by connected demand and supply elements. Therefore, for every material/plant combination, you will see a detailed sequence of requirements and receipts over the time which respects the segmentation strategies. In detail, you will see the pegging quantities per receipt or requirement item.

Image 12. Example of ALV display with overview tree of material/plant combinations for MD04P.

The MD04P report displays a user-friendly ALV screen, so it can be transferred to Excel in tab file format. The ALV display list maps the appropriate stock elements to the requirement elements for the individual stock segments and displays them in separate tabs according to the MRP statuses. The system considers the segmentation strategy in the mappings.  Specifically, MD04P considers the rules maintained in the “ARun/MRP: Requirement/Stock Allocation” rule for mapping the requirement to stock.

The main screen has several tabs and an optional details screen like the one in MD04. On the details screen, an overview of the material master data is also available for the material/plant combination, by using the button to expand and collapse the material header details data.

Image 13. Example of ALV display with expanded header area for MD04P.

The main screen displays the information about how stock/supply elements are planned to be used (result of MRP run). This screen is separated into different tabs according to the MRP statuses, and the main information available is:

  • The free available stock (non-consumed)
  • The blocked requirements (non-assigned)
  • The consumed stock (assigned requirements and consumed stock)

The free available stock is the valid available stock that has not yet been assigned to any requirement. The blocked requirements are any demand that has not yet been covered by any existing stock. The consumed stock indicates for which demands the stock is planned to be used and what requirement segment they have.

Image 14. Example of ALV display with stock/supply elements grouped by MRP status for MD04P.

The main information displayed on the different tabs is the following:

  • Tab “A – Free Available Stock” shows the valid available stock (batch stock, purchase requisition, planned order, production, and so on) that has not yet been assigned to requirement.
  • Tab “B – MRP Blocked Requirements” shows any demand/requirement that has not yet been covered by any existing stock.
  • Tab “T – Temporarily Assigned Stock/Requirements” shows the list of the requirement elements against stock elements (catering elements) in one line. After MRP run, the stock element is updated for every requirement element. The other planning details like dates and material are also available on the same line.
  • Tab “X – Excluded requirements” shows requirements that are not considered during MRP in a make-to-stock scenario, for information purposes only.
  • Tab “∑ – Stock/Requirement Overview” gives more details about the requirement element. Summary of information available from all the tab strips.
  • Tab “I – Additional Information” gives the summarized details of the stock elements and required elements. In this view, users can see the overall picture for the planning situation along with excluded stock for planning.

You can navigate to the corresponding documents in display or change mode based on the MRP element type. For example, if the MRP element is “PrdOrd”, you can double-click the requirement number and navigate to the corresponding Production Order (display transaction CO03 and change transaction CO02).

Other functions are also accessible from the top menu of MD04P report, like order conversion or release purchase requisition. Navigation to the Display Total Requirements and Stock Overview screens is also possible.

Image 15. Example of functions accessible from the top menu for MD04P.

When executing the MRP Live (transaction MD01N) on foreground mode, you can also navigate to the Stock/Requirements Pegging List (MD04P) besides the Stock/Requirements List (MD04).

Image 16. Navigate to MD04P as result of MRP Live execution.

MD04P report contains some specific features (for instance, seasons) which are only available for the industry solution SAP S/4HANA for Fashion and Vertical Business. They are only displayed with specific fashion transaction FSH_MD04.

 

Behavior of Stock/Requirement List (MD04) with Segmentation

When the Stock/Requirements List (MD04) is executed for a segmented material, the requirement segment is in the stock/requirement list table’s last column, and the stock segment is in the header level right after the plant code. This means that you can check all the stock elements and their pegged requirements for a single stock segment, which may be linked to the same or a different requirement segment.

The complete overview can be loaded for a material and plant for all stock segments or a specific stock segment. If a specific stock segment is entered as a filter on selection screen, only supply elements with that stock segment are listed.

Image 17. Example of MD04 display for segmented material.

 

Custom Fixed Pre-Assignment Tool

Functionality of Supply Assignment (ARun) can be activated by a business function and provides an optimized assignment of existing stock to open requirements by creating a fixed pre-assignment that remains till outbound delivery is created. In a supply shortage situation, supply pre-assignment ensures an optimal assignment of supply to the open requirements and distributes available supply to the respective demand (sales orders, stock transport orders, etc…)

The stock/requirements pegging list (MD04P) is enhanced with Supply Assignment (ARun) status details on separate tabs, according to the different ARun available statuses. Here a list with all the pre-assigned stock batches to the different requirements is displayed.

Customer was not only interested in pre-reservations against stock batches, but also in pre-reservations against planned orders and pre-reservations against production orders. Moreover, greater flexibility for manual assignations was also requested. Therefore, the true challenge was

  1. To develop a new app for fast manual linking of stock and receipt elements with requirement elements, whilst strictly respecting the segmentation mapping rules.
  2. To enable the MRP Live for not considering the already pre-assigned requirements and stock/receipt elements for the netting calculation (the so-called fixed elements or fixed pegging).
  3. To integrate the already fixed pre-assigned requirements-stocks into the stock/requirements pegging list.

Therefore, a new app called “Fixed Pegging Tool” was created for allowing a fast manual linking of stock/receipt elements with requirement elements, whilst strictly respecting the segmentation mapping rules.

Image 18. Selection screen of Fixed Pegging Tool.

Image 19. Main screen of Fixed Pegging Tool.

The main screen of the new app shows some header information and two main ALV displays separated by vertical splitter. The left ALV displays the requirement elements grouped by temporarily assigned and already pre-reserved requirements. The right ALV displays the stock/receipt elements.

By clicking on a single requirement segment, the cell becomes green color and valid stock segments based on the segmentation mapping rules are also highlighted on green color, thus helping the user to identify the possible valid lines to be linked to particular requirement.

Image 20. Valid stock segments for single requirement segment on Fixed Pegging Tool.

On the other hand, by clicking on a single stock segment, the cell becomes green color and valid requirement segments based on the segmentation mapping rules are also highlighted on green color, thus helping the user to identify the possible valid lines to be linked to a specific batch or planned order or production order.

Image 21. Valid requirement segments for single stock segment on Fixed Pegging Tool.

Once the user has decided the records to be linked, buttons “Link Orders” and “Unlink Orders” can be used to link left with right records or unlink them.

Image 22. Buttons for linking and unlinking orders on Fixed Pegging Tool.

When one requirement is linked, the old MRP status “Temporarily Assigned Requirements” becomes into the new status “Reserved Requirement” on MD04P (fixed element).

Image 23. Example of linked requirement with production order on Fixed Pegging Tool.

We could establish again a parallelism between the pegging information derived from segmentation and the fixed pegging derived from custom pre-assignment tool with the dynamic and fixed pegging procedures used for establishing pegging relationships in PP/DS. Dynamic pegging would correspond to the pegging information derived from segmentation while fixed pegging would correspond to fixed pre-assigned information coming from the custom tool.

Technically, the reservation takes place on RESB table with an append structure containing the planned order or production order number, as batch number is already available by standard.

Image 24. Example of linked requirement with production order on RESB table.

The already fixed pre-assigned requirements are displayed in the stock/requirements pegging list (MD04P) on existing tab “R – Supply Assignment: On Reservation”. The reserved stock element is also displayed and “Element is fixed” column is marked. This is achieved by implementing BAdI BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_AMDP.

Image 25. Example of linked requirement with production order on MD04P.

Finally, BAdIs BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_APPL and BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_UI are also available. With the first one, you can add new fields or change any existing fields that are relevant for the stock element or the requirement element in the stock/requirements pegging list. With the second one, you can define the display layout such as field catalog and sorting criteria for each of the MRP statuses (tabs) of the stock/requirements pegging list.

 

Helpful Links

Monitor Stock/Requirements List (SAP Fiori Apps Library)

Stock/Requirements Pegging (SAP Help Portal)

Incident component for clarifications:

  • Transaction MD04P: LO-RFM-PP
  • Supply Assignment (ARun): LO-RFM-ARN

For further details on SAP S/4HANA segmentation, you can also refer to the SAP Press E-Bite "Introducing Supply and Demand Segmentation in SAP S/4HANA".

 

[Disclaimer: Written by Juan Ventura (LinkedIn). This blog post is written by the author based on his personal experience while working on customers with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Opinions and views expressed herein are solely my own. Before implementing any solution, you are advised to always do the analysis and adopt a solution for specific business case only if it suits customer requirements and testing is successful.]

entry.title: Ready, Set, Demo: SAP Business One Releases Updated Localized Demo Databases
entry.content:
We’re happy to announce that the localized demo databases for SAP Business One have been refreshed and upgraded to the newest version, 10.0 FP 2602. These updated databases are now ready for download!

This release includes newly generated transactional data, giving your demos a clean, modern, and polished appearance.

The localized demo databases deliver a complete, pre-configured SAP Business One environment across 39 different localizations, all built on the OEC Computers model company. They are offered for both MS SQL and SAP HANA deployments.

Whether you’re presenting SAP Business One, conducting training, exploring product capabilities, or performing tests, this updated demo environment helps you work with more accuracy, clarity, and real‑world relevance.

Download the latest SAP Business One Localized Demo Databases

entry.title: SAP Public Cloud 2602 / Uninterupted Workflow for Parked Invoice as Complete
entry.content:
Hello Experts,
i have a question regarding the Configuration of the Workflow for Parked Invoice as Complete (WS00800303).
I have it setup as follows:
1. One Employee creates the Supplier invoice and parks it.
2. The second employee has to check the invoice and make changes if necessary.
3. The third employee makes a final check and posts the invoice

The issue for me is, that currently if the employee in step 2 or step 3 makes a change to the supplier invoice and parks it again, the workflow gets canceled and then restarts from the beginning. 
My question is, is there a way to configure the workflow in a way that the change to the invoice doesnt restart the complete workflow and it just goes to the next employee ? 

Best regards
Lukas

entry.title: Re: Output Management - Send Billing Document via email b...
entry.content: Hello Izabellelopes, is there any news? As menioend, the above solution does not work. My understanding is, it is not possible to maintain a characteristic for the business partner that serves to distinguish the output channel.

entry.title: IDocs vs Event-Driven Integration in SAP
entry.content: We are currently using IDocs for system integration in SAP and are exploring the possibility of moving to an event-driven architecture using SAP Event Mesh. How can IDocs be replaced with events in such a scenario, and what are the recommended best practices for implementing event-driven integration in SAP?

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 5: Innovations in SAP Integrated Business Planning
entry.content:
This fifth post in my series gives an overview of UX innovations that came in the last six months, with the SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) releases 2508, 2511 and 2602. In many areas Joule and embedded AI helps users with their planning, with explanations and guidance. We have also focused on improving usability of applications, usability of the SAP Fiori launchpad, as well as features for collaboration and Microsoft Teams integration.

In case you missed it, Part 1 provides general updates about Joule, SAP Work Zone and SAP Mobile Start.

I’ve grouped the UX innovations into these main sections:

  • Joule
  • AI-assisted features (embedded)
  • SAP Fiori launchpad
  • UX enhancements in applications
  • Collaboration enhancements (Microsoft Teams)

Let’s first look at how Joule is using AI to help users, before continuing with the embedded AI and the AI-assisted features.

 

Joule

Joule: AI-Assisted Supply Optimization Analysis

Joule helps planners to quickly understand the results of supply optimizer planning, and boosts their productivity by allowing easy comparison of different planning scenarios. Not only does this make their work easier, it also leads to a reduction in lost sales and inventory costs.
Planners can ask Joule about their supply planning runs, including time series optimization. Joule analyzes the planning run logs and provides explanations for missed demand fulfillment, inventory targets, and manual adjustments, as shown in Figure 1. Joule also addresses identified issues and provides suggestions for resolving them. It can also compare different planning runs to give additional insights.

Figure 1: SAP Integrated Business Planning, AI-assisted supply optimization analysis. Alt-Text: Screenshot of Planner Workspace, with four images of Joule overlayed: first the user asked “Provide the status of the last planning run for planning area XAP4”, after seeing the status summary asked “Why the demand fulfillment rate low”, resulting in an analysis, then asked “Summarize the KPIs”, resulting in a tabular response, and then “What are the mitigation options to resolve demand fulfillment for PRODUCT02 and Customer 101?”, resulting in detailed analysis.

Watch it in action and find out more:

  • Video:  AI-Assisted Supply Optimization Results Analysis in SAP IBP - 2511 Release Highlights & Demo.
  • SAP Discovery Center: SAP Integrated Business Planning, supply optimization analysis.
  • Documentation: AI-Assisted Supply Optimization Analysis.

Simplified Job Scheduling with Joule

You can execute jobs and monitor them without having to leave their application – simply by asking Joule. You can list your saved jobs with a simple ask and schedule jobs directly through Joule. You can also check job statuses, including your own jobs, failed jobs from today, or jobs created by colleagues. Figure 2 shows an example.

Figure 2: Job scheduling with Joule. Alt Text: Screenshot of Intelligent Visibility showing a world map, with four images of Joule overlayed. The user prompted “I would like to run my saved job”, Joule asks to select one, the user selects “Forecast to Loads”, is shown the details and says “Run the job”, finally receiving a success illustrated message.This is interactive planning, reimagined for speed and simplicity.

  • Video: Job Scheduling with Joule in SAP IBP - 2508 Release Highlights & Demo (1:47 minutes).

Using Joule to Run Master Data Health Checks

Users can now ask Joule to run a master data health check for any master data type they specify, as show in Figure 3. In the past, health checks could only be started from the Manage Master Data app or using the Master Data Health Check application job, but as of SAP IBP 2508, users can define their query in a conversation with Joule and read the job results in a result table displayed in the Manage Master Data app.

Figure 3: Running master data health checks with Joule. Alt Text: Two screenshots, each with Joule in foreground. 1) User prompts “In my last supply planning run, I had some errors that may originate from master data inconsistencies. I suspect the error is in ZP1LOCATION. Could you please check it?”. Joule replies that it found 16 messages, here are the first 10, listing them in a three column table. 2) “Can you please also check ZP1LOCATIONPRODUCT?”, result: illustrated message “no inconsistencies found”.

 

AI-Assisted Features (Embedded)

We have introduced a number of AI-assisted features, embedded in applications, that help planners save time in their daily work: AI planning assistance helps them create formulas and formatting rules, the other analysis features help planners understand the outcomes of the often complex planning algorithms, as well as making recommendations for even better outcomes.

AI Planning Assistance

AI planning assistance is now generally available with the 2602 release for SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP), add-in for Microsoft Excel, having benefitted from feedback from our customers during beta-testing these last six months.

Planners can now easily generate formulas and formatting rules for their planning views, by simply saying what they need using natural language. This makes their lives much easier, since in the past creating formulas was complex and often required an administrator.

Figure 4 shows an example for generating formulas, where the user first asks to calculate deltas, upon which new rows for the deltas are added. Next, the user asks to add totals per year, resulting in new columns being added for each year.

Figure 4: Generating formulas using natural language with AI planning assistance. Alt Text: two pairs of images showing planning sheet and bar chart, first with prompt pop-over, then with resulting update to the sheet, as described in the text.

In addition to creating formulas, planners can use natural language to highlight critical values in their plan, making it easy to identify aspects that need further attention. Figure 5 shows an example query.

Figure 5: Formatting data using natural language with AI planning assistance. Alt Text: screenshot of part of a planning sheet with 8 rows below a bar chart, with a pop-over for entering the instruction “Show a red font in case capacity supply expansion is greater than capacity supply”.

This generative AI feature streamlines the planning process, empowering planners to focus on adding value rather than spending time on technical details.

To see this in action and find out more, have a look at:

  • Video: AI-Assisted Generation of Formulas (4:52 minutes).            
  • Video: AI-Assisted Generation of Formatting Rules (3:23 minutes).
  • Documentation: Planning Assistance and General Availability of Planning Assistance.

AI-Assisted Forecast Results Analysis

Making forecasts with advanced models is powerful, but the outcomes are not necessarily intuitive and easy to understand. This is where SAP IBP, AI-assisted forecast results analysis helps users.

You can now display detailed explanations for values of the main forecast key figures. This can help you understand how each algorithm in the forecast model calculated the forecast, how the best-fit algorithm was selected, and how you could enhance parameter settings to improve forecast accuracy. Figure 6 shows an example.

These explanations are available both in the Planner Workspaces app and in the SAP IBP, add-in for Microsoft Excel.

We continue to enhance the scope of what the analysis can explain. With the 2602 release, it can explain the impact of independent variables, offsets, and system-generated features selected in the forecast model.  Also, both Statistical Forecast Details and the assisted forecast results analysis includes product lifecycle information. In addition to that, you can now navigate from Statistical Forecast Details to the application log and forecast model that are relevant for the selected forecast results.

Figure 6: SAP Integrated Business Planning, AI-assisted forecast results analysis. Alt Text: Screenshot of Planner Workspace, with a section on the right supporting vertical scrolling containing sections for Summary, Recommendation (both visible), Forecast Algorithms and Parameter Explanation.

Find out more:

  • Video: AI-Assisted Forecast Results Analysis (2:36 minutes)
  • Documentation: Check Statistical Forecast Details in Planning UIs and Enhancements to Statistical Forecast Details in Planning UIs.

AI-Assisted Inventory Optimization Analysis

Inventory optimization algorithms can also be challenging to understand, which is where our AI-assisted inventory optimization analysis comes in: with this, inventory planners can save up to  25% of their time on analyzing optimizer run results!

It summarizes key reasons for the recommended safety stock output values and adjustments made, impacting final safety stock for use in downstream processes. Demand variability, lead time fluctuations, service levels, and other relevant modeling inputs are highlighted as key influences, alongside planner deviations from recommendations. Figure 7 shows an example.

Figure 7: SAP Integrated Business Planning, AI-assisted inventory optimization analysis. Alt Text: Screenshot of Planner Workspace, with the bottom half containing AI-Assisted Inventory Analysis, containing sections for Summary, Analysis (both visible), and Diagnostic Key Figures.

With the 2602 release, it now also provides insights into how the immediate upstream and downstream nodes in your supply chain network impact the safety stock levels for the product-location you're analyzing. This means that you can better understand safety stock drivers across multiple stages of your supply chain.

Find out more:

  • Video: SAP Integrated Business Planning for inventory (2:11 minutes).
  • Documentation: AI-Assisted Inventory Analysis and What’s New in 2602: Analyzing the Network of Safety Stock Results with AI-Assisted Inventory Optimization Analysis.

AI-Assisted MRO Inventory Analysis

In addition to the AI-assisted analysis for inventory used for production and sales, with the 2602 release we now provide SAP Integrated Business Planning, maintenance, repair, and operations, AI-assisted MRO inventory analysis. With this, MRO planners can get a 30%​ reduction in time spent on analyzing inventory runs.

With just one click, the new AI-Assisted MRO Inventory Analysis gives the planner a concise summary and intelligent analysis of MRO inventory planning runs, accompanied by a table that highlights the inputs and settings considered, as shown in Figure 8.

It is available in both Planner Workspaces and in the SAP IBP, add-in for Microsoft Excel, and you can review operator settings and configuration on the same screen where planning occurs.

Figure 8: SAP Integrated Business Planning, maintenance, repair, and operations, AI-assisted MRO inventory analysis. Alt Text: Screenshot of Planner Workspace, with the bottom half containing AI-Assisted MRO Inventory Analysis, containing sections for Summary, Analysis, General Settings (all three visible), and Key Figures.

Watch it in action and find out more:

  • Video: AI-Assisted MRO Inventory Analysis in SAP IBP – 2602 Release Highlight & Demo (2:05 minutes)
  • Documentation: AI-Assisted MRO Inventory Analysis.

SAP Fiori Launchpad and Design Updates for Suite Consistency

We have made design improvements which are relevant for a consistent user experience across the entire SAP Business Suite, and which are now also available with SAP IBP. As I mentioned in my April blog post Upcoming Design Updates for the SAP Business Suite (SAP Fiori for Web), consistency of the user experience across the various applications used by a user is an important part of overall usability, helping users to feel confident and at ease when using a suite of products – no unexpected surprises.

In this section, we’ll look at these updates (the first two cover the design updates for consistency):

  • New navigation menu, with direct access to favorite apps.
  • Updated design of notifications and user menu.
  • Launch apps directly via transaction code.
  • Update for administrators

Navigation Menu: Side Navigation

Many SAP products already offer a navigation menu on the left of the screen, a pattern already familiar to many people. Hence we have decided to apply that approach to navigation across the SAP Business Suite in upcoming releases.

In SAP IBP the side navigation is displayed as an overlay, as you can see in Figure 9 on the left. It gives you direct access to your favorite apps, either directly or via the folders you have created. It also gives you direct access to your Spaces and Pages. The previously available menu to access all your spaces and pages along with personalization options is available via the entry “All Spaces” at the bottom of the menu.

A big benefit of this new navigation menu is that you can now access your favorite apps directly from any application. Previously, you would have to return to My Home and then click on your favorite app, now you can access it directly from the menu, as shown in Figure 9.

Figure 9: New navigation menu and updated design of notifications and user menu. Alt Text: 3 screen snippets showing each of the menus as a pop-over.

Updated Design of Notifications and User Menu

The new simplified design of notifications shown in Figure 9 fits better with the overall design of SAP IBP, and provides simplier interactions. By default notifications are sorted by date, with grouping by “Today”, “Yesterday”, “Last Week”, “Last Month”. Users can also choose to sort by importance.

The new user menu design shown in Figure 9 provides more space for the avatar of the user, and provides more space between items for easier selection. The settings are always at the top as the first entry, and the “Sign Out” option is separated from the actual navigation options in the list, to make it easier to find.

Launch Apps Directly via Transaction Code

I already covered this new feature in detail in part 2 of my series, since the launchpad is used by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 too. Rather than repeating it here, I suggest that you look at the examples in the corresponding SAP Fiori launchpad subsection of my part 2 blog post:

  • Launch apps directly via transaction code.

Find out more:

  • Documentation: What’s New: Launching an App with its Transaction Code in SAP Fiori Launchpad and Searching and Launching Apps.

Administrators can Upload and Download Spaces

In the Manage Launchpad Spaces app of the SAP Fiori launchpad, administrators can download existing spaces together with their assigned pages and upload them again to another system.

This enables you to transfer spaces and their assigned pages to other systems, for example, when you can't use transports, meaning that you can improve productivity and consistency across systems. Find out more:

  • Documentation: Downloading and Uploading Spaces

 

UX enhancements in applications

We have introduced two very significant innovations that provide a much better user experience, but without dramatically updating the actual UIs! This is because user experience is so much more than just the UI: if a user can get more done than before, with less effort, achieving better ourcomes, then that is a definite improvement in their experience with the system.

The first of these, introduced with the 2508 release, is the harmonized planning area. The second, introduced just now with the 2602 release, is harmonized scenario management. In both cases, the “harmonized” in the name signifies that these new capabilities unify and simplify how users work.

Beyond these, we have introduced a number of user experience enhancements for planning UIs and for master data management, which are covered below too.

Harmonized Planning Area

The harmonized planning area lets you use one planning area to plan both mid- to long-term supply, demand and inventory with time-series buckets and short- to mid-term supply with orders.

Even better, it integrates both live (real-time) and scheduled data flows using the same data model, i.e. attributes, master data types, key figures, planning levels, time profile etc.

With integrated, harmonized tools and processes, planners can smoothly adopt additional SAP IBP capabilities, leading to better reporting and planner efficiency. Improved flexibility in time-series supply planning helps planners quickly adapt to changing demands and market conditions. Centralized application mapping and name space management as well as a new central unit of measure and currency conversion help to streamline configuration. Additionally, planners have on-the-fly access to order key figures, enabling more informed decision-making.

Find out more and watch a demo here:

  • SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) 2508 - Available Now!
  • Getting Started with SAP IBP New Harmonized Planning Area (SAPIBP2)
  • Video: Harmonized Planning Area in SAP IBP – 2508 Release Highlights (5:28 minutes).

Harmonized Scenario Management

Available with the 2602 release, harmonized scenario management in SAP IBP unifies and simplifies how you work with scenarios across orders and key figures, letting you manage the full lifecycle in Planner Workspaces: create, modify, simulate, and promote both order and key figure data within one framework. It extends scenario lifetimes across orders and key figures and adds new promotion options for orders. Controlled shine-through now includes orders, giving you better control of how baseline changes propagate.

Together with the harmonized planning area, it advances integrated planning so orders and forecasts work seamlessly without separate processes. Look at an example and find out more:

  • Video: Harmonized Scenario Management in SAP IBP - 2602 Release Highlight & Demo.
  • Documentation: Harmonized Scenarios.

Planning UI Enhancements

Enhanced Filtering of Workspaces and Segmentation Variants

Introduced with the 2508 release, users can explore data sets more easily now by leveraging segmentation variants. This provides a third level of filtering flexibility for users. The first two options were available previously, the third one is new:

  1. Manually define filter values for the data
  2. Use filter variants to store combinations of filter values for reuse, so that you just choose the variant rather than manually maintaining all the filters.
  3. Define segments using attributes like “Product ID” and “Customer ID” with which you can step through the filtered data from (2).

The new segmentation variants are helpful when a selected filter variant results in a large list of data. Without the segmentation variant, users would still have to do a lot of scrolling. This targeted approach significantly reduces data volume, enhances speed and focus, and delivers a smoother experience to your planning process. Have a look at how it works:

  • Video: Segmentation Variants in Planner Workspaces in SAP IBP - 2508 Release Highlights & Demo. (2:26 minutes)

With the 2602 release, we have further enhanced filtering in the Planner Workspaces app. You can now:

  • Manage planning filters directly in Planner Workspaces, including the options to share, edit, delete, and assign groups to existing filters.
  • Overwrite an existing planning filter when you create a new planning filter with the same name.
  • Use advanced operators to define attribute-based filters in many areas across Planner Workspaces, as well as in URL-based navigations to workbooks and variants.
  • Filter aggregated constraint key figures by the (Not Specified) attribute value.

The advanced operators improve usability significantly: you can use “starts with”, “contains”, “greater than” to quickly get to the data you are interested in. Figure 10 shows an example. You can find our more here:

  • Documentation: Enhancements to Filtering in Planner Workspaces.

Figure 10: Filtering with advanced operators in the Planner Workspaces application. Screenshot of Planner Workspace with filters Time Periods “Next 10 weeks”, Product Group “SMART*” and Location Desc “*DC*”. The list at the bottom shows six entries fulfilling the filter criteria.

Navigate Directly to Root Cause of Planning Obstacle

SAP IBP 2508 enhanced the Planner Workspaces application so that users can navigate directly from an identified issue to the root cause, be it a gating resource or component constraint.

Dedicated planning views provide a clear overview of supply and demand dynamics at these critical bottlenecks, empowering you to make informed adjustments. By encompassing both gating resources and components, this feature accelerates root cause analysis and resolution improving planning efficiency and decision-making. Figure 11 shows an example.

Figure 11: Navigate directly to root cause of planning obstacle within the Planner Workspaces application. Alt Text: Screenshot of Planner Workspace with an open context menu next to a cell in the Demand sheet with a red number. The entry “Show Gating Resources” is highlighted in the menu, with an arrow pointing down to a second sheet at the bottom of the screen showing Capacity.

Easy Scenario Comparison with Scorecards in Planner Workspaces

Scorecards let planners compare what-if scenarios across multiple KPIs to select the best option. Introduced in SAP IBP 2508 and enhanced in 2511, they support up to seven KPIs, each based on a key figure. For each KPI, you can set filters and define a direction: “Bigger is better”, “Smaller is better”, or “Within a range”. Figure 12 shows an example where three scenarios are being compared.

Figure 12: An example scorecard for comparing scenarios in Planner Workspaces. Screenshot of Planner Workspace with a pop-over “Compare Scenarios with Scorecard” showing columns for three scenarios Baseline, Promotions, and Promotions wo AIR, and a list of 7 KPIs for each of them. The baseline column is a different color than the others.

When a key figure appears multiple times, you can rename KPIs for distinct views (e.g., Capacity Supply for Assembly vs. Sub-Assembly). You have a lot of flexibility in how you visualize the comparisons: you can compare scenarios by absolute or percentage differences, and also swap rows and columns. You can also promote the optimal one from the scorecard.

Find out more here:

  • Video: Scorecards in Planner Workspaces in SAP IBP - 2511 Release Highlights & Demo (3:10 minutes).
  • Documentation: Working with Scorecards.

Further Usability Enhancements in Planning UIs

Have a look at the What’s New documentation for further usability enhancements with the 2602 release:

  • Usability Enhancements for SAP IBP, Add-in for Microsoft Excel.
  • Enhancements to Planner Workspaces.

Smarter Master Data Management with New Application

The new version of the Manage Master Data app delivers enhanced functionality with focus on planning area scope and master data relationships. Users can explore comprehensive parent-child connections and drill down into dependencies for thorough impact assessment. The app enables personalized experiences through customizable columns, groupings, and sorting based on parent master data attributes.

Teams can collaborate more effectively by sharing standardized views through public variants, all while benefiting from robust, fine-grained access control through planning area-specific authorizations that ensure security across all operations.

Find out more here:

  • Video: New Manage Master Data App in SAP IBP - 2511 Release Highlights & Demo (1:57 minutes).
  • Documentation: Manage Master Data (New Version).

Collaboration Enhancements (Microsoft Teams)

In addition to the built-in collaboration enhancements mentioned above (for example, in the new master data application), we have invested in real-time collaboration with messaging into Microsoft Teams.

Real-Time Microsoft Teams Messaging for Planners

Planners can now send real-time messages to Microsoft Teams channels directly from SAP IBP when saving key figure data or scheduling application jobs, sharing reason codes, comments, and contextual details. They can select the target Microsoft Teams channel directly, either in the SAP IBP, add-in for Microsoft Excel (see Figure 13), or in the Planner Workspaces application.

This reduces communication delays and ensures transparency across planning teams, especially for those colleagues and stakeholders who aren’t regular SAP IBP users.

Planners also benefit by having less manual effort, since they don’t have to create separate nofifications or e-mails.

Figure 13: Real-time messaging for planners via Microsoft Teams. Alt Text: Two screen snippets: 1) a pop-over for “Save Key Figure Changes”, with a field at the bottom “Share With” containing the entry “Sales and Operations Team/General”. 2) a pop-over showing Microsoft Teams General channel for Sales and Operations Team, with a comment in the thread.

Integration of Microsoft Teams for Jobs

Also new with the 2602 release, you can have jobs automatically post comments to Microsoft Teams channels to indicate when a job step starts running. This ensures that everyone is kept up-to-date in real time, including those colleagues who are not regular users of SAP IBP. Read more here:

  • What’s new documentation: Integration of Microsoft Teams for Jobs.

 

Further information

For information about all the new enhancements for SAP IBP, have a look at these blog posts and the links therein:

  • SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) 2508 - Available Now!
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) 2511 - Available Now!
  • SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) 2602 - Available Now!

 

Continued soon …

I hope you enjoyed this fifth part, covering the new UX innovations available for SAP Integrated Business Planning. I will post part six in the next few days, covering exciting new UX innovations available for spend management.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

entry.title: Re: SAP Business One System Requirements for HANA and SQL...
entry.content:
Hi Javokhir,

for SAPB1 on SAP HANA, 64 GB of RAM is an absolute minimum for services operate properly. The more data and users on HANA the more RAM. SAPB1 on SQL is not that resource-hungry.

entry.title: Re: Need to know the impact of Following 102 mvt with Goo...
entry.content:
If your organization strictly avoids modifications: Stick with the current procedure. Educate users that once UD is made, reversal is not possible, and corrections must be handled via subsequent postings (e.g., return to vendor, adjustment postings).

If operational flexibility is critical: Implementing Note 175842 can help, but you must weigh the long-term maintenance cost. Every upgrade or patch may require reapplying or adjusting the modification.

Governance is key: If you do implement, restrict access to reversal transactions, and document the business justification clearly for auditors.

entry.title: Re: Need to know the impact of Following 102 mvt with Goo...
entry.content:
movement type 102

    in MIGO.
  • This works even when inspection types (like 04) are active.
  • However, once a usage decision (UD) is made and stock is posted (e.g., 321 from quality to unrestricted), the reversal becomes problematic:
  • Standard SAP does not allow reversal of goods movements triggered by UD.
  • This can lead to inconsistencies between inspection lots and stock postings if users manually reverse GR without aligning the inspection lot status.
  • SAP Note 175842 introduces functionality to reverse goods movements created from usage decisions(e.g., 321, 101) It ensures that:
    • The inspection lot and related stock postings are reset to the status before the first posting.
    • The Quality Management Information System (QM-IS) is updated accordingly.
  • Limitation: This is a modification, not a standard delivered feature. SAP explicitly states it is not part of the standard system.
  •  
  • If your company has strict “no modifications” policy, then
    do not implement Note 175842
      . Instead:
      • Train users to avoid reversing GR after UD is made.
      • Use QA12 (reset usage decision) and manual corrections where necessary.
    • If audit compliance and data integrity are critical, implementing the note provides a cleaner solution but at the cost of introducing a modification.

    Stick to best practices (no modifications) unless your business faces frequent audit issues or operational risks due to mismatched inspection lots. In that case, escalate to SAP to check if a standard solution or enhancement exists in your release before resorting to Note 175842.The note itself is still classified as a modification 

       SAP explicitly states it is not part of the standard system and requires manual changes to enable reversal of goods movements created from usage decisions.
    • There is no newer standard note that directly replaces 175842 with delivered functionality. SAP has not incorporated this reversal logic into the core system in later releases.
    • The official recommendation remains: avoid modifications unless business-critical, and instead use standard processes (resetting usage decision, manual corrections).

    • Standard Alternatives (Without Modification)

      SAP provides some standard ways to handle reversals without implementing 175842:

      • Reset Usage Decision (QA12): This reopens the inspection lot so you can adjust postings manually.
      • Manual Stock Adjustments: Reverse stock postings using standard movement types (e.g., 102 for GR reversal, 321 for quality to unrestricted), but this requires careful reconciliation with inspection lots.
      • Business Process Controls: Train users not to reverse GR after UD is made, and instead handle corrections through QA12 and subsequent postings.

 

Practical Guidance

  • If your organization has a strict “no modifications” policy, you are aligned with SAP best practices by not implementing 175842.
  • The impact of continuing with 102 reversals is mainly around data integrity and audit risk:
    • Stock postings may not match inspection lot status.
    • Quality records may show UD completed even though stock was reversed.
  • If your business faces frequent reversals after UD and this causes operational or audit issues, then 175842 could be justified but you’d need to accept the risks of a modification (upgrade impact, additional testing, maintenance overhead).

advice: Stick with best practices (no modifications) unless reversals after UD are a recurring, business-critical issue. In that case, escalate to SAP for guidance on whether newer enhancement options (BAdIs, user exits) can achieve similar functionality without a full modification.

entry.title: Localization in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
In today's interconnected world, running a global business can feel like navigating a vast ocean. Each country is a new port with its own unique set of rules, regulations, and customs. With the 2602 release, we're not just giving you a better map; we're upgrading your entire navigation system, ensuring you can sail smoothly and confidently, no matter the destination.

Let’s dive into the exciting new features that will help you go global while staying perfectly local.

  • A Single Source of Truth: The Intuitive Tax Register
  • Mastering Global Complexity: Country-Specific Highlights
  • Universal Innovations for Global Business
  • AI campaigns

A Single Source of Truth: The Intuitive Tax Register

Imagine a world where your tax data isn't scattered across different ledgers and reports, but lives in one, centralized, and transparent place. This world is here with the new Tax Register for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This is a true game changer for tax accountants.

We are providing a single source of truth for all your indirect tax operations. This powerful tool allows you to:

1) Visualize the entire lifecycle of each tax line, from posting to reporting and payment.
2) Gain centralized insights and a real-time view of your tax position, company wide.
3) Increase flexibility by making tax operations independent from accounting.
4) Effortlessly reconcile and ensure you are always audit-ready.

Initially rolling out for VAT in Germany, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Oman, the Tax Register is your new command center for managing tax payables and ensuring comprehensive compliance.

  • Display Tax Items (ID: F4756):
    • View and analyze posted tax items with tax-specific attributes.
    • Track the lifecycle of tax items.
    • Navigate to related documents for detailed insights.

Display Tax Items (ID: F4756)

  • Manage Tax Payable (ID: F4755)
    • Create, post, and cancel tax payable documents.
    • Automatically generate documents when reports reach Content Accepted by Government status.
    • Option to manually create tax payable documents if required.

Manage Tax Payable (ID: F4755)

  • Migrate Tax Items(ID: F6715): Migrate tax items posted prior to Tax Register activation from the standard database table (BSET) to the new table (TAX_REG_ITEM) for reporting and analysis.

 

   Migrate Tax Items (ID: F6715)

Mastering Global Complexity: Country-Specific Highlights

Every region has its own complexities, and our 2602 release delivers targeted solutions to make your life easier.

Turkey: Taming Foreign Exchange Gains

Navigating the nuances of foreign currency in Turkey just got a whole lot simpler. Businesses know the challenge of the (Kur fark) foreign exchange gain invoice required when exchange rates shift between invoicing and payment. Our update automates the creation of billing documents for these gains in Convergent Invoicing. The system automatically calculates the gain, ensures correct VAT treatment, and generates the necessary invoices and accounting documents, saving you invaluable time and minimizing errors.
(Kur fark) foreign exchange gains

Brazil: Unprecedented Flexibility and Extensibility

Brazil's fiscal landscape is famously dynamic, and we're putting more power than ever into your hands. This release introduces a suite of enhancements for Brazil, including:

a. Self-Service for External Tax Engines: A new configuration interface allows you to map custom tax type codes to an external tax engine, enhancing flexibility in sales processing.
b. Extensible Nota Fiscal Models: You can now extend and adapt nota fiscal models for sales processes, allowing you to meet specific requirements not covered by the standard version.
c. Multiple Account Assignments: We now enable external tax calculation for multiple account assignment distribution during invoice verification, enhancing customer satisfaction and accuracy.

Universal Innovations for Global Business

Compliance is a global theme, and some of our most powerful new features are designed to work across borders.

1) Business Partner Certificates (Cross-Country): Managing customer certificates and their validity dates is a critical compliance task. You can now configure and maintain these certificates directly in the business partner master data, making the information readily available for processes like invoicing and payments. This improves compliance and reduces manual effort across all your regions of operation.

Business Partner Certificate

 

2) Expanded Scope Item Availability (Cross-Country): Our global family is always growing! We are thrilled to announce the availability of more scope items for dozens of SAP-delivered standard local versions. From Argentina to Australia, from Canada to Colombia, and from Singapore to South Africa, we are making it easier than ever to steer your business operations globally using SAPs standard, automated, and compliant processes.

AI campaigns

We’re excited to invite you to participate in the beta testing program for innovative new capabilities:

  1.  AI-Assisted Incoming Bills of Exchange Posting. Ready to get involved? Click the Register button at the top left to secure your spot. Together, let's make handling Bills of Exchange more efficient and straightforward!  
  2. EASYFIT: AI-Assisted Easy Entry of Treasury Financial Transactions: Get a preview of how AI can simplify the process of recording financial transactions in Treasury and Risk Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition while ensuring compliance with country specific regulations.

Delta localization information on new, changed or deleted features as well as legal changes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 for your country? Check the Help documentation country/region specific section What’s new

Check Localization Community

The 2602 release for Localization is more than just a list of features; it's our steadfast commitment to helping you navigate the complexities of global commerce with confidence and ease. We handle the local complexities so you can focus on what you do best: grow your business.

I hope you're as excited about these updates as I am! To learn more, I encourage you to explore the SAP Help Portal and join the conversation on the SAP Community.

📽️ Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12-16, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP Cloud ERP 2602 release. Missed the live sessions? We've got you covered! Take advantage of the entire series or pick and choose the sessions most relevant to you. You will find the recording and presentation for Localization as well as 21 other topics of interest. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

🔎 Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below: 

2602_Enablement-wheel

entry.title: Re: Process Integration Pipeline Extension - Restart via Data Store
entry.content:
Hi Raj,

One of the main reasons we developed this process is the lack of JMS queue APIs. There is no way to control how messages are written to the JMS queues nor any way to read from them.

With the 2513 release, the JMS queue monitoring UI now shows Sender, Receiver, Application Message Type and Application MessageID with filtering also supported. But there is no automatic way to reprocess messages in an automated and/or scenario-specific way (like in PO the restart job) once the messages move to a JMS DLQ nor any APIs to achieve this requirement.

Additionally we think that routing error should not be retried, as they'll fail unless someone fixes the routing.

In summary, the reason we developed this extension to achieve automatic and on-demand restart capability as it's currently not possible to do out-of-the-box.

Regards,
Mate

entry.title: Getting started with the SAP Logistics Management setup
entry.content:
I am sure that by now, as an avid Supply Chain professional,  the announcement of SAP's latest solution to enrich its Digital Supply Chain portfolio has not gone unnoticed. Of course, I am referring to SAP Logistics Management.

Although being a nascent product, I will let you be the judge, but I am sure you will find the SAP Help page provided to aid you in the setup and running of the solution to already be extremely complete and rich. That said, as with any product help, there is a limit to what and how much can go into it. This blog aims to complement, not replace the provided SAP help, with visual aids to hopefully help the new comers to the SAP world as well as provide tips to maybe the more seasoned professionals amongst you. 

This blog will cover some of the key SAP Logistics Management setup tasks - that you do not want to go wrong.

Where appropriate, I will for the sake of continuity reference the relevant SAP help page.

Setting up your Organisational Structure

As with any system, there must be structure in the house (an action or transaction will occur in a structuring company level or a logical combination of many). The logistical levels of your company must be mirrored in your ERP and in SAP Logistics Management (referred to as LGM hence forth). Unless you are embarking on a green field implementation, it is quite likely that you will already have defined some of those elements, such as 'Company Code', 'Sales Area', etc.. The point of this section, will not be to cover all SAP organisational levels, but only those that are critical to be able to execute your logistics processes across your ERP and LGM.

Setting up your Organisational Structure in ERP 

I say ERP, but at the time of writing this blog I mean SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (aka Cloud ERP Private, S/4HANA PCE..), (Please check this page for minimum release requirements). Note: This blog will not cover the creation of an entire Organisational Structure (company code, shipping point, sales area, etc...) which are all needed. This blog will only focus on the key organisational levels required to create a warehouse in LGM.

This step will essentially entail defining a third party  warehouse. To do so you will need to leverage /create 3 SAP organisational objects:

  • A Plant
  • A Storage Location
  • A Warehouse (number)

Once you have created those organisational elements, you will then need to indicate how they relate to one another and also let your ERP know that the warehouse you have created is managed -externally- to your ERP (your inventory value and quantity will be managed and visible in ERP at the IM -Inventory Management- level, but managed and tracked in quantity down to the warehouse bin level in LGM).

The steps required to define a third party warehouse are detailed here in the LGM SAP Help.

Define a Plant

LGM can be deployed in a green or brown field implementation scenario. Thus it may be that you already have a plant defined in your system that you simply wish to extend to be used with LGM, or you need to create a new one to coincide maybe with the creation of a new warehousing site.

If you need to create one, then go to your ERP implementation guide (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Logistics - General --> Define Plant

Enter the Plant details, such as (1) the plant code and name and (2) address details) and save your entries when you are done.

Defining a Plant

I will not show these steps in detail, but also make sure to complete the assignments of your plant to company code (Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Logistics - General --> Assign plant to company code) , the assignment of a shipping point to your plant (Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Logistics Execution  --> Assign shipping point to plant) and the assignment of a purchasing organisation to your plant (Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Materials Management  --> Assign purchasing organization to plant). 

Define a Storage Location

To create a storage location, then go to your ERP implementation guide (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Materials Management --> Maintain Storage Location . The storage location is hierarchically below the plant, so before being able to define a storage location, you first have to indicate the plant in which you want to create this storage location. We will use the plant code previously created.

We will then select the 'New Entries' button and add (1) our storage location code and description and then (2) the address of the storage location. Remember that this storage location, will effectively be a warehouse (as I am using a demo system I have given the description 'LGM WHouse L10' for my storage location and referenced a warehouse number L10 even though I have not yet created it, simply to know that it is mirrored in LGM !).

Define a Warehouse Number

The last organisational object we need to create is a Warehouse Number (that's all it is. A number and a description). To create a warehouse number, then go to your ERP implementation guide (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Logistics Execution --> Define Warehouse Number . 

We will then select the 'New Entries' button and add our warehouse number and description.

 Now that we have created all the organisational objects we need, we can now proceed to representing how they relate to another.

Assign Warehouse number to Plant and Storage Location

As said, a warehouse number, on its own is just that. So we need to indicate to ERP, what this warehouse number maps to physically - a combination of a plant and storage location. To do so go to your ERP implementation guide (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Logistics Execution --> Assign Warehouse Number to plant and storage location. 

We will then select the 'New Entries' button, and indicate the three objects we have previously defined, namely (1) the plant code, (2) the storage location code and (3) the warehouse number. We have now mapped our plant/storage location to our warehouse.

 

Defining the warehouse as third party (external) warehouse

The last step on the ERP side, is to define the warehouse as being 'externally to the ERP' managed. This effectively to indicate to ERP, that when we transact in ERP in the plant/storage location that corresponds to a warehouse number, a message must be sent to the warehouse number to advise it to complete a logistical movement at the warehouse level.

To perform this step, go to your ERP (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Logistics Execution --> Decentralized WMS Integration --> Central Processing --> Application --> Activate Decentralized WMS.

There, for your warehouse number, follow the instructions from the SAP help and make the settings as below.

 

Other -optional- ERP settings

I am in this section going to share with you other settings that you may need to make, depending on your scenarios. 

It may be, especially early on in your implementation project or as you test the solution that you want to update the stock in LGM, by performing an initial 561 (initial stock entry) or 501 (Receipt w/o PO) goods movement in your ERP to perform an IM (plant/storage location) movement, in order to be able to process a putaway (at warehouse level) in LGM. In order to facilitate this 'handshake' between ERP and LGM, ERP will automatically attempt to create an inbound delivery, upon saving the goods entry in ERP, but it needs some information to do this. Namely it requires a Business Partner representing the plant, acting as the 'Supplier' (moving stock from IM to warehouse level) and it requires certain delivery copy control settings to be in place.

Define Default Values for delivery

Once you have created a business partner to represent your plant as the 'Supplier', then go to your go to your ERP (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Logistics Execution --> Decentralized WMS Integration --> Central Processing --> Application --> Inventory Management Interface and Delivery-Relevant Data --> Define Default values for deliveries.

 Then for (1) your warehouse number, indicate (2) the business partner that represents the plant and maintain the (3) logistics execution and (4) sales area information.

Copy Control for Deliveries

Upon processing a 501/561 stock movement you may get an error message saying  'Copy control is missing for: DZL DIG'. If you do, then you need to maintain additional copy control settings for deliveries. To do so go to your ERP (transaction SPRO), and then follow the path SAP Customizing Implementation Guide --> Logistics Execution --> Shipping --> Copying Control --> Specify Copy Control for deliveries . Transaction VTLA will also take you there directly.

Once there, select 'New Entries' and maintain the information as below:

Header level:

 Item Level:

 

Setting up your Organisational Structure in LGM

Now that we have defined our warehouse in ERP, we can pivot to setting it up in LGM. The relevant LGM help page for this is here. What you will notice is that there is no app in LGM that allows you to manually create any organisational object one by one, like you have in ERP or perform some kind of API sync to replicate the organisational structure from ERP. Instead, you have to perform an spreadsheet based import and upload.

NOTE: I would at this point be remiss, as indicated in the SAP help, to not remind you of an important warning before you proceed here. Whilst the 'Import Organizational Model' app performs certain checks during the import of a file, you cannot remove erroneously created organisational objects or relationships once they are created in LGM, nor is there a check against ERP to check that an imported object really exists in ERP (beware of typos! - so make sure the values you use are strictly identical to those from the source ERP system and have a second set of eyes gloss of over your upload file).

The procedure to follow in order to import your organizational structure into LGM is well detailed here. In a nutshell, you need to use the 'Import Organizational Model' app to (1) download a template spreadsheet with some sample data, amend it with your own data, and then (3) import it back into the same app, in order to (4) create your structure in LGM. There is also a dedicated help page that provides you with more details on the information you need to provide in the template for each object (you need to click on an object, then go down to the bottom of the page to see the details!).

You may also consider, as you move your project from Development --> to Test --> to Production,  to use the (2) export/import features in the 'Import Organizational Model' app to move a cleansed organisational structure down the line (rather than re-creating a new file every time).

Once you have imported your structure into LGM, a graphical representation of it is available for viewing in the 'Import Organizational Model' app.

 Handy tips in the 'Import Organizational Model' app

Using the 'Import Organizational Model' app should be fairly straight forward, but I will below highlight a couple of points that may be of use.

formOfAddress

In the 'ShippingPointAddresses' tab of the template, there is a tab named 'ShippingPointAddresses', and within it a (1) field named 'formOfAddress'. The sample file will be populated with the value '0002', and you may be wondering what this value corresponds to, and what other choices there are !

To find out the list of possible codes and their meaning, then you need to open the app (1) 'Display Code Lists' and see (3) possible values for the item (2) 'Forms of Address'.

As a preamble to this step note that your LGM system will initially be devoid of any code lists, nor do you have the ability to enter any manually in LGM. In order to have your LGM system populated with code lists, then you must perform the Code List Replication step which is detailed here in the SAP help. This step replicates these code lists directly from your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition system, by calling an API thus ensuring that your ERP is the single source of truth (and avoids double maintenance!).

As a sidenote, in order to replicate these code lists, you must choose the replication object 'SAP S/4HANA Customizing Data' during replication.

 

Handling localised texts

There is a reference to this in the SAP help here.

It may be that you are a multi-lingual organisation, and need to maintain texts for organisational objects in different languages. If that is the case, then you can maintain language specifics in the tab named 'LocalizedTexts' of the template, but you need to make sure that you reference the correct object using the correct naming convention. We will take the hypothetical example to localise a company code. 

My starting point is the template, where I have indicated the details below for a company code 1010, that is in ERP defined as being geographically located in Germany (Country_code = DE). The name, in English (my user language) of this company code is (1)  'Company Code 1010'. I have also attracted your attention to the language code (de = German) , only to say that this is the language of the company (it does not mean that 'Company Code 1010' is the German name of the company!). 

 Before I move on from this tab, I note the following information, as I will need it to localise it.

  • (2) The object type (the entityName). The template allows me to localise all organisational objects, so I need to be able to indicate what object it is that I am referring to that I want to localise. Every sheet will in the top left have a unique code. In this case it is 'CompanyCodes'.
  • (3) The object to localise. I could in my template have provided the data for multiple company codes, so I need to be specific about which company code it is that I want to localise. This is the code in the column named 'displayID'. In this case I refer to company code 1010.
  • (4) The term to localise. We have to be specific and be able to tell which of the fields it is that I want to localise. In this case the term I want to localise is the information in the column named 'name'.

So now that I have the information I need, I can move to the sheet named 'LocalizedTexts' and see how we put this together! I have added details for other object types (entityName), but the first highlighted line is the one of interest for our company code.

 In this sheet, we carefully add the values we recorded in the 'CompanyCodes' sheet and add the missing pieces, namely:

  • (4) The locale - the 2 digit language code of the localised language (the language for which we are providing a translation)
  • (6) The value - the localised text. The translated text.

Once I have imported the template in to LGM and to make sure that my data is correct, we can do a check using the 'Import Organizational Model' app , by connecting in the appropriate language (go into the Fiori Launch Pad 'Me' menu, then choose 'Settings' and 'Language and region'.

Manage Warehouse Layout app

If you are like me, once the setup was done, I was eager to start setting a default warehouse in my user preferences, only to have a moment of panic when I saw, that at this point none could be selected! 

 So to avoid checking and double checking what could have gone wrong, first go to the 'Manage Warehouse Layout' app and make sure you have maintained the 'Manage Warehouse Attributes' of your warehouse! Once this is done, you should be able to see your warehouse appear in the various drop down lists in LGM. The reference to the SAP help for this section is here.

 

Importing Dimensions and Units of Measure

The LGM SAP help for this section can be found here and is quite comprehensive.

At the end of this step, you will have imported into LGM Dimensions and Units of Measure information from your ERP. In order to do this you will need to download some templates from LGM, extract some data via SE16 from your ERP, populate said templates and upload them into LGM. The help link above provides you all the information you need in order to perform this step (which applications to use, which tables to extract data from..) I will in this section merely provide you with some tips to hopefully help you speed up your workflow.

Before you begin, make sure to make note of the caution to not select the 'Respect Conversion Exit' option when you use SE16 in ERP !

Also, to avoid extracting incomplete datasets from ERP, make sure to (1) check how (2) many entries your tables contain and adjust the (3) Maximum number of hits accordingly.

 

Export/Import Dimensions data 

This step entails extracting data from tables T006D and T006T (for the localised texts) in your ERP.

To facilitate and minimise the amount of data massaging you will need to do, I would suggest you adjust the output columns to line up with those available and required by the template. For table T006D, I narrowed down my list of fields to the below - showing how they map to the template (as the templates may change over time, make sure to check in your environment before proceeding - this is your responsibility). I then extracted the SE16 table data set to Excel and copy/pasted the content in the template - making spot checks to make sure everything lined up and was accurate!

Note that I did not maintain any texts here.

 As my LGM system is intended to be used for English and German speakers, I need to extract Dimensions texts in both these languages.

As per the help, I query table T006T for this. Note: As languages tables can be quite voluminous depending on the number of languages maintained in your ERP, make sure to restrict the search to the languages of interest.

 This table will be a little tricky to handle as the columns from the SE16 Extract do not line up exactly like the template, and the language code from the SE16 extract is one character long, but the template requires 2 characters. Things to note are :

  • That some (1) texts are fixed and required by the template (make sure every line has this)
  • You should have a dimension repeated as many times as you have selected languages
  • Use the Search and replace functions of excel to mass search and replace language 'E' for 'en' and 'D' for 'de' in the locale column.

Again make spot checks to make sure the data you will upload is accurate and complete (as the templates may change over time, make sure to check in your environment before proceeding - this is your responsibility)

Export/Import Units of Measure data 

This step entails extracting data from tables T006 and T006A (for the localised texts).

As before I will show you the column layout I selected and how I arranged it so that it mapped with the template file, allowing me to do a copy/paste operation. Again make spot checks to make sure the data you will upload is accurate and complete (as the templates may change over time, make sure to check in your environment before proceeding - this is your responsibility).

The Units of Measure file with the localised texts is probably the trickiest one to handle, so make sure to check your data set before uploading it to LGM. I say tricky because the SE16 extract will be a flat linear data extract that requires to be transposed for the upload template. I have tried to explain this with the diagram below. In other words you will for each 'keyValue' have to maintain the 'commercialCode', 'shortText' and 'longText' on different lines. At the risk of repeating myself,  make spot checks to make sure the data you will upload is accurate and complete (as the templates may change over time, make sure to check in your environment before proceeding - this is your responsibility).

 That's it for this time folks.

Stay tuned for more content on LGM, and don't hesitate to drop requests  for future blogs in the comments whether it pertains to setup or processes!

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud 2602 / Uninterupted Workflow for Par...
entry.content:
Dear @LukasKuhn,

In Public Cloud flexible workflow what you see is expected behavior:

  • The workflow is started by the Save as Complete (park-as-complete) event
  • If someone edits the invoice and then parks / saves as complete again, SAP cancels the running instance and starts a new one so that start/step conditions and approvals are re-evaluated on the changed document. SAP even describes edit -> park ->save as complete as the way to restart the workflow

So there is no standard configuration that will say after a change, do not restart just continue to the next approver and keep the already completed steps as-is

SAP Notes below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002931104

https://me.sap.com/notes/0002996515

What to do instead for example move the editing outside the approval workflow

1. Employee 1: Creates the supplier invoice and parks it (NOT Save as Complete). I'm making sure you're using Park in this step?

2. Employee 2: Opens the parked invoice in Supplier Invoices List makes corrections and only then clicks Save as Complete

3. Employee 3: Performs final check and posts approval/post step in the workflow

This way changes in step 2 don’t cause a restart because the workflow hasn’t started yet

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003579989

Best Regards,

Dawid
