entry.title: Implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in an SAP Environment
entry.content:
Summary

This article outlines the solutions available within the SAP ecosystem for implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). RAG enhances generative AI applications by grounding them in your specific, private business data, ensuring responses are accurate, relevant, and trustworthy. We will explore three primary architectural patterns, detailing their use cases, technical components, and the criteria for selecting the most suitable option for your enterprise needs.

Business Requirements

Modern enterprises require generative AI solutions that can:

  • Provide answers based on internal, up-to-date business data rather than generic, public information.
  • Understand and query unstructured text data (e.g., material descriptions, service notes, emails) based on semantic meaning, not just keywords.
  • Accurately retrieve facts from structured data where complex relationships are critical (e.g., supply chain dependencies, product configurations).
  • Offer a rapid, low-maintenance path to enable question-answering over existing document repositories (e.g., PDFs, Word files).
  • Ensure enterprise-grade security and governance, controlling how and where sensitive business data is processed.

Solution Options

SAP provides a flexible set of capabilities on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to meet these requirements. The primary options are:

  • Option 1: Custom RAG with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  • Option 2: Custom RAG with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph
  • Option 3: Managed RAG with the Document Grounding service on SAP BTP

Characterization of the Solution Options

Option 1: RAG with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine

This approach is ideal for building RAG applications on unstructured or semi-structured text data. It gives you maximum control over the process by leveraging the native vector capabilities within SAP HANA Cloud.

  • How it works: Text data (like product descriptions) is converted into numerical representations called vector embeddings, which capture its semantic meaning. These embeddings are stored in SAP HANA Cloud using the REAL_VECTOR data type. When a user asks a question, the application finds the most conceptually similar text chunks in the database using functions like COSINE_SIMILARITY and provides this context to the Large Language Model (LLM).
  • Architecture: The core is SAP HANA Cloud. To generate the vector embeddings, you can choose from three patterns:
    1. External Model: Use an embedding model from the Generative AI Hub, a third-party provider, or a model you host yourself. This provides flexibility in model choice.
    2. Integrated NLP Server: Use the local embedding model within SAP HANA Cloud’s NLP service. This is a highly secure option as your data does not leave your HANA Cloud instance.
    3. SAP AI Core Integration: Connect SAP HANA Cloud to an embedding model deployed on SAP AI Core for on-the-fly generation.

 

Option 2: RAG with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine

This option is designed for structured data where the relationships between data points are essential for answering a query correctly.

  • How it works: Your business data is modeled as a graph of entities and relationships (e.g., a material is produced by a manufacturer, which is located in a specific country). When a user asks a question, it is first translated into a SPARQL query to retrieve precise facts from the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph. These factual results are then given to the LLM to generate a fluent, natural-language answer.
  • Use Cases: Answering complex queries about supply chains, bill of materials, organizational structures, and other highly interconnected data.
  • Architecture: Requires SAP HANA Cloud with the Knowledge Graph feature enabled. Your application architecture must include a component to translate natural language into SPARQL queries, which can be facilitated by libraries like the langchain-hana connector.

Option 3: Managed RAG with the Document Grounding service

This is a managed, low-code approach for enabling RAG on document collections, abstracting away the underlying complexity of the vector pipeline.

  • How it works: You upload your business documents (e.g., PDFs on a file share) to the Document Grounding service on SAP BTP. The service automatically handles document parsing, chunking, embedding, and indexing. When you make a call to an LLM via the orchestration service, it automatically retrieves relevant passages from your documents to ground the response.
  • Use Cases: Quickly building a chatbot or Q&A application over a repository of contracts, policy documents, or technical manuals.
  • Architecture: This solution is built on SAP BTP and is accessible via Joule as well as the Generative AI Hub. In the Joule integration, it is possible to connect selected document repositories directly to Joule to make them accessible in the Joule Copilot. For custom applications, Grounding in the Generative AI Hub requires a subscription to SAP AI Core Extended Plan and ideally the AI Launchpad. The infrastructure for ingestion, indexing, and retrieval is fully managed by SAP.

Criteria to determine the most suitable target solution

To select the best-fit RAG solution for your specific scenario, consider the following key criteria. These questions are designed to help you evaluate the three options against your business and technical requirements, and they form the basis of the evaluation spreadsheet linked in the next section.

1. Primary Data Type and Structure

What is the primary format and structure of the data you need to use for grounding?

  • Is it unstructured or semi-structured text residing within database tables (e.g., material long texts, service notes)? This points toward Option 1: SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine.
  • Is it a collection of separate document files (e.g., PDFs, Word documents, PowerPoints) stored in a repository? This suggests Option 3: Managed RAG with the Document Grounding service.
  • Is it highly structured data where precise, multi-step relationships are critical (e.g., supply chain networks, bills of material, organizational charts)? This indicates a strong fit for Option 2: SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine. 

2. Required Query Sophistication

What kinds of questions will your users ask?

  • Are they seeking information based on semantic similarity and concepts in business data (e.g., "find products similar to a laptop" or "summarize past maintenance notifications relating to pump failures")? This is a core strength of Option 1.
  • Are they asking for factual answers that require traversing complex relationships (e.g., "which suppliers in Europe provide parts for product X")? This requires the relationship reasoning capabilities of Option 2.
  • Are they asking questions about the content within a specific set of business documents (e.g., "what does our policy say about remote work")? This is the primary use case for Option 3. 

3. Implementation Speed vs. Customization Control

What is the priority: rapid time-to-market with a managed service, or fine-grained control over the RAG pipeline?

  • For the fastest path to a solution with minimal infrastructure management, choose Option 3.
  • For maximum control over the embedding model, chunking strategy, and retrieval logic, choose Option 1 or Option 2. 

4. Security and Data Governance

What are your data residency and processing requirements?

  • If you have a strict requirement that your data must not leave the SAP HANA Cloud instance during the embedding process, the most secure pattern is Option 1, using the integrated NLP server.

5. Target Application and Integration Context

Is the solution for a fully custom application or for integration with a standard SAP tool?

  • If the primary goal is to make documents accessible to an end-user copilot, the Joule integration with the grounding service (Option 3) is the most direct path.
  • If you are building a bespoke generative AI application, all three options are viable, with Option 1 and Option 2 offering the deepest integration possibilities for custom logic.

6. Team Skillset and Resources

What is the technical expertise of the implementation team?

  • A managed, low-code approach (Option 3) is best suited for teams that want to focus on the application logic rather than the underlying AI pipeline.
  • Building a solution with the Vector Engine (Option 1) or Knowledge Graph Engine (Option 2) requires development skills in data modeling, SQL, and potentially SPARQL, making them suitable for teams with deeper database and application development expertise.

Decision Tree

The following decision tree provides a visual guide to selecting the most appropriate RAG solution for your needs:

 

Conclusion

Implementing RAG in an SAP environment is crucial for creating intelligent, context-aware business applications. SAP provides a powerful and flexible toolkit within the AI Foundation on SAP BTP to achieve this. The choice between using the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine, the Knowledge Graph, or the managed Document Grounding service depends directly on your specific data, the complexity of your use case, and your operational preferences. By carefully evaluating your business requirements against these solutions, you can build a robust architecture that securely grounds your generative AI applications in the data that runs your business.

entry.title: Discover and Publish Events from SAP Advanced Event Mesh in Developer Hub
entry.content:
Introduction

Event-driven architecture (EDA) is a cornerstone of modern integration, enabling systems to react in real time to business events with loose coupling and scalability. In this blog, we walk through how to connect SAP Advanced Event Mesh with SAP Developer Hub to discover and publish events, empowering integration teams and developers to explore and reuse event streams within the SAP ecosystem.This setup is especially relevant if you’re looking to build responsive integrations or microservices that react to business events across SAP solutions.

🟢 Delivered Roadmap Item

SAP has delivered manual discovery and publication of events from Advanced Event Mesh to SAP Developer Hub a...

For a complementary scenario focused on discovering events from SAP S/4HANA Cloud using SAP Developer Hub, see the blog “Discover S/4HANA Cloud Events in SAP Developer Hub.” That post walks through registering your S/4HAN...

 

1️⃣ Overview: Why Discover and Publish Events

  • Developer Hub acts as a central catalog for APIs and events.
  • Advanced Event Mesh is the cloud service that enables event distribution across hybrid landscapes.

Discovering events helps teams understand what events are available and reuse them consistently.

2️⃣ Set Up the Destination to Connect Event Mesh to Developer Hub

  1. Go to SAP BTP Cockpit → Subaccount
  2. Navigate to Connectivity → Destinations → Create from Scratch
  3. Fill in details:
    • Name, Type: HTTP
    • Base URL of Advanced Event Mesh
    • Authentication (e.g., BasicAuthentication with API token)
    • Custom property: sap.isuite.dh.aem.enabled = true

3️⃣ Register and Configure Your Advanced Event Mesh Instance

Describe how to ensure Event Mesh and its event APIs are set up for discovery.

  • Create event APIs or ensure existing ones are shared
  • Enable appropriate access permissions

Note: Only shared events will show up in Developer Hub

4️⃣ Discover Events in SAP Developer Hub

Steps to explore events once the destination is set:

  1. Open Developer Hub via Integration Suite
  2. Go to Admin Center → Manage → Content
  3. Select your Advanced Event Mesh business system

Browse events grouped by application domain

5️⃣ Create Products and Publish Events

Explain how to group events into products and publish them:

  1. In Developer Hub, select events you want to publish
  2. Create a Product (logical grouping of events)

Summary

In this blog you learned how to 

  • Connect Advanced Event Mesh with SAP Developer Hub
  • Discover and publish events for reuse
  • Enable developers to consume events in integration use cases
  • Publish the Product to make it discoverable by developers

entry.title: Re: Setting up Currency Exchange Rate API in Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Hi,

we had the same ambition to connect to the service via OAuth, but couldn't achieve it. Ultimately we simply did it X.509 certificate based.

I guess a reason could be the following:

Communication scenarios that support OAuth 2.0 as authentication method for inbound communication can now be consumed by public OAuth clients using PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange). This allows desktop applications (acting as OAuth Clients) to consume OAuth-protected OData services with the identity of a business user. The business user will be prompted for providing consent to this action once (a web browser is required for this).

If PKCE based OAuth communications requires actively providing consent via a user action, it won't fit into a backend based communication scenario. I am certainly not an OAuth expert, but the above is my explanation why it doesn't work in this context.

Best regards, Stefan

entry.title: Leveraging Past Technical Dialogues to Enhance Customer Support with SAP BTP
entry.content:
When a client opens a ticket for SAP software support into AMS services, time to resolve remain a relevant KPI.  S.P.A.R.K. (SAP Process Augmented Response Knowledge) is a Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG) platform built entirely on SAP technology—SAP HANA Cloud, BTP AI services, and the Cloud SDK. It turns every resolved ticket into a searchable, conversational knowledge base, delivering the exact solution a consultant needs exactly where they work. 

In this blog we’ll show how S.P.A.R.K. overcomes the challenges of technical dialogue data, why a native‑SAP stack matters, and the measurable impact on ticket‑resolution speed and consultant productivity. We’ll also highlight the scientific paper that documents the architecture and results—our first peer‑reviewed publication made possible thanks to SAP and Talan Research Center.  

This blog post describe technical implementation and research outcomes led within SAP AMS delivery center at TALAN. 


The Challenge: Unlocking Conversational Data 

Unlike standard document retrieval, support tickets are messy. They are conversational, often asynchronous, and the critical information (the root cause and solution) is diluted across many messages. Furthermore, AMS support tickets present specific hurdles:

  1. Data Privacy: Tickets contain sensitive info like client names and system IDs which must be protected. 
  2. Technical Jargon: Standard models struggle with the specific vocabulary of SAP as transactions and error codes. 
  3. Asymmetry: We need to match a new problem description against past resolved tickets that contain both the problem and the solution. 

 

The Solution: A Secure, High-Performance RAG Architecture 

To address these challenges, we designed a robust pipeline on SAP BTP that transforms raw noise into actionable insights. As illustrated in the figure below, the SPARK architecture follows a strict sequence to ensure data privacy and relevance. Whether processing historical archives (Database) or a live request (New Ticket), the data passes through 3 main steps.

 1. Anonymization (Privacy Layer) 

Before any data is processed by external AI models, it must be sanitized to protect sensitive information. 

The system uses metadata (knowing who the client, consultant, and company are beforehand) to apply Regular Expressions (Regex). This systematically strips out names, first names, company names, and other specific identifiers. 

To comply with legal confidentiality requirements and prevent "bias" in the similarity search (e.g., preventing the model from linking tickets solely because they share a company name rather than a technical issue). 

2. Summarization (Resume Layer) 

Raw conversation logs are often noisy, containing greetings, scheduling details, or digressions that dilute the technical content. 

The data is sent to a Large Language Model (LLM) via the SAP GenAI Hub. The system uses a specific prompt to force the LLM to structure the output into three distinct sections: 

  1. PROBLEM: A description of the initial issue. 
  2. LEADS (Pistes): The diagnostic steps and hypotheses considered. 
  3. SOLUTION: The final resolution and implementation details. 

The LLM  generates synthetic data to augment and refine the initial dataset. By doing so, it enhances consistency, semantic relevance, and overall coherence, ensuring that the data better aligns with the target application. 

3. Encoding (Vector Layer) 

This step converts the text summary into a format that computers can compare mathematically. 

The text is passed through an Embedding Model (such as OpenAI's text-embedding-ada-002 or others tested like SAP NEB). This model converts the text into a high-dimensional vector (a long list of numbers). 

To store these vectors in the SAP HANA Vector Engine. When a new ticket arrives, its vector is calculated and compared against stored vectors using Cosine Similarity to instantly find the "N closest tickets" based on semantic meaning rather than just keyword matching.

 

All‑In‑One SAP‑Native Stack: The Technical Engine Behind the Solution 

The solution is built entirely on a SAP‑native stack: all ticket texts are stored in SAP HANA Cloud, leveraging its in‑memory columnar engine for ultra‑fast reads, writes, and auditability. 

 Embeddings are generated and indexed by the HANA Vector Engine, which performs approximate‑nearest‑neighbor similarity searches directly inside the database, eliminating any external vector store and keeping latency in the single‑digit‑millisecond range. 

For the generative layer, SAP AI Core on the Business Technology Platform orchestrates any LLM whether open‑source models or SAP‑tuned variants through a unified runtime that handles model loading, scaling, and secure inference & GPU/CPU provisioning. 

The entire pipeline from vector retrieval to prompt engineering and response generation is exposed via the SAP Cloud SDK, ensuring a single, secure endpoint for consultants while keeping all data within the trusted HANA vault and benefiting from SAP’s built‑in compliance and monitoring capabilities. 

 

Results: Finding the Perfect Model Combination 

We evaluated dozens of model variants on a 100 thousand item dataset by relying on real‑world feedback from SAP consultants. Each answer was manually annotated as relevant, partially relevant, or not relevant, and those human judgments were used to rank and refine the models. This human‑centered evaluation ensured that the final solution meets the practical needs of consultants. 
 
From a research perspective, we quantify this relevance with the classic Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG) metric, a staple in information‑retrieval evaluation. Using nDCG we compared several system configurations, manual prompting versus the automated “PromptPerfect” approach, and the impact of an additional Re‑Ranking (RR) step. The metric lets us clearly see how each variation lifts the relevance of the top‑ranked answers, guiding data‑driven decisions on which pipeline components to adopt. 

 

 According to these tests, the most effective configuration achieved a relevance score of 0.86. Interestingly, the best results (highlighted above) came from a "hybrid" approach: combining Gemini 1.5 Pro (accessed via GenAI Hub) for generating the summaries, paired with OpenAI’s Ada 002 model for generating the text embeddings. 

This proves the value of SAP GenAI Hub: it allows developers to easily swap and combine models from different providers (like Google, Anthropic or OpenAI) to optimize for specific tasks without changing the underlying architecture. 

 

Acknowledgments 

We are grateful to the research community for the peer‑reviewed paper that introduced this work. You can read the full publication (in French) here: 

https://aclanthology.org/2025.jeptalnrecital-industrielle.6.pdf  

A sincere thank‑you to SAP for providing the end‑to‑end platform SAP BTP — SAP HANA Cloud, Vector Engine, AI Core, and Cloud SDK—that made the solution possible. Your technology and support have been essential to turning academic insights into real‑world value for SAP consultants.

entry.title: Re: Service entry issue.
entry.content:
Root Cause

  • Both invoices were linked to the same PO.
  • The service entry quantity for both invoices was mistakenly posted under one invoice.
  • Payment has already been settled, so the PO quantity is fully consumed, blocking the second invoice.

Solution

1. Reverse the Incorrect Service Entry Sheet

  • Use MR8M (Invoice Cancellation) or ML81N (Service Entry Sheet reversal) depending on your ERP setup.
  • Cancel the wrongly posted invoice/service entry.
  • Recreate the service entry sheet correctly, splitting the quantities between both invoices.

2. Create a Credit Note

  • If reversal is not possible (because payment is already settled and period is closed), raise a credit note against the first invoice.
  • Adjust the PO/service entry quantities accordingly.
  • Post the second invoice with the correct service entry.

3. Manual Adjustment via PO Amendment

  • If reversal/credit note is not feasible, request the procurement team to amend the PO quantity/value temporarily.
  • Post the second invoice with a new service entry.
  • Then reconcile the PO back to its original state.

4. Finance Adjustment 

  • If system restrictions prevent corrections, handle the adjustment in FI (Financial Accounting):
  • Post a manual journal entry to offset the overcharged invoice.
  • Record the second invoice separately for audit compliance.

Hello Experts,

We are facing an issue in SAP Analytics Cloud starting from 10th February.

Our report uses a script on the table on result changed event and Batch Export to generate PDFs. Until 9th February, it was working correctly.

Now:

The table data changes correctly for each Tract during Batch Export.

However, the text boxes are not updating.

The values shown on the first page of the PDF are being repeated on all subsequent pages.

No changes were made to the model, script, or report configuration from our side.

Could you please confirm if there was any update or patch released around 10th February-2026 In SAP SAC that might have impacted Batch Export or scripting behavior?

Thanks in advance.

Sumit

entry.title: Re: One Delivery Order with multiple PO numbers generates...
entry.content:
Dear @JasClemencio,

Question 1.

Multiple customer PO numbers in one delivery do not automatically force an invoice split. The split occurs if billing header split-relevant fields are determined from values that differ per item/order. The most typical split triggers are:

  • Reference field on the billing header (FI Reference / VBRK-XBLNR) if configured to be derived from Customer PO number then different POs = different header value = billing split is required because the billing header can only hold one reference value
  • Assignment number on the billing header (FI Assignment / VBRK-ZUONR) same logic if derived from a value that differs by item/order, it can force a split
  • Different header partners (payer/bill-to) or key header conditions like payment terms / incoterms / currency / company code coming from different underlying sales documents. Split analysis will tell you exactly which header partners/fields differ

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3009009

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2800221

Also sanity-check that the split isn’t self-inflicted by the billing run:

  • In Create Billing Documents app, if someone selected Create separate billing document for each item of billing due list, SAP will split even when consolidation would otherwise be possible. SAP Note 2800221 above

Question 2.

Point A. Split Analysis first?

SAP explicitly recommends running split analysis to identify the differing header partners / header fields that caused the split

SAP Notes below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3009009

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2800221

Point B. Review Copying Control for Billing Documents in CBC

In Public Cloud what you’d call VTFL on-prem corresponds to the SSCUIs for copying control:

  • ID 102762 Define Copying Control for Sales Document to Billing Document
  • ID 102763 Define Copying Control for Delivery Document to Billing Document
  • ID 102764 Define Copying Control for Billing Document to Billing Document, if relevant

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3269803

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3009009

In those steps, check how the system derives billing header fields, especially:

  • Reference number determination the Note lists options like Customer PO / Sales order / Delivery / External delivery / Current invoice
    If you currently copy Customer PO into the billing header reference then multiple POs in one delivery will naturally split

Point C. Check whether any custom data transfer routine is influencing header fields

If you have any custom logic review Badi SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER because it can change header data in a way that triggers splits. SAP points to this Badi as the supported enhancement point for influencing billing document creation / data transfer

Question 3.

Usually yes if the split is caused by how header fields are being filled especially Reference/Assignment.
The standard fix is to ensure that the fields SAP uses at billing header level are not derived from values that differ across the delivery items like multiple customer PO numbers

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3009009

Important. The billing header has only one customer PO / reference type value. So if the business expects one invoice and still wants to see all PO numbers the typical standard approach is:

  • keep one consolidated invoice and
  • show Customer PO per item or as a list in the output/layout

If the business requires the billing header to contain a concatenated list of multiple PO numbers not just item-level display that typically becomes an enhancement topic via SD_BIL_DATA_TRANSFER + custom field because standard header fields won’t store multiple PO numbers cleanly

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2800221

Summary:

  1. Run Split Analysis between the two invoices and note the exact field(s) that differ
  2. If it’s Reference / Assignment adjust ID 102762/102763/102764 so those fields don’t derive from Customer PO or derive consistently and re-test
  3. Confirm nobody is using separate billing doc per due list item in the billing run settings

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: MyInbox App in Work Build Zone only scrolls to currently selected item
entry.content:
The following behavior is currently persistent in our MyInbox in the Build Work Zone:

- We have selected an item from the list which is either a) not at the top of the list or b) will not be at the top of the list after a refresh.

After pressing the refresh button, the "All tasks" bar doesn't slide to the top to showcase the latest document that has come in or may be hidden there - this is indicated in the screenshot below. So there may always be any items at the top of the list which we don't see.

We are calling this in our code by 

this.getInboxAPI()?.updateTaskList();

This is internally somehow delegated to 

 

this.getOwnerComponent().getEventBus().publish("com.sap.spa.inbox", "refreshListInternal")

The question now is, do we have any further control over this?

entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)
entry.content:
PLEASE NOTE: Selecting the ‘Will you be attending?’ option will NOT register you for the event.

To register for this event, please click here: Register Here

SAP SuccessFactors: Build your Adoption Program​ to Maximize Results (English; Europe & Americas)

This session will help you understand the relevant components of an adequate adoption program to maximize your cloud investment and ensure sustained business value. We will walk you through the essentials to orchestrate Change Management, Governance, Sustainment, and Value Management and how they work together to drive ongoing adoption, solution optimization and user experience.​ Participants will explore best practices and take away actionable templates to build an initial transformation roadmap, define clear roles and responsibilities, and identify the right executive sponsors and stakeholder groups.​ This webcast is a foundational session that will lead into optional follow‑on topic specific webcasts on your preferred topics.. At the end, you will leave with a concrete structure and toolkit to start or refine your own SuccessFactors adoption program.

SuccessFactors Onboarding@sap.com for any questions on this event.

Here is a link to our Global schedule
